title: Employees Above Average Salary
problem_statement:
Find all employees whose salary is strictly above the company average.

Return name and salary, ordered by salary descending.
hint: Use a subquery with AVG().
solution_sql: SELECT name,salary FROM employees WHERE salary>(SELECT AVG(salary) FROM employees) ORDER BY salary DESC;

title: Department Highest Salary
problem_statement:
Find the employee with the highest salary in each department.

Return dept, name, salary ordered by salary descending.
hint: Use a subquery with MAX() grouped by dept, then join back.
explanation: Eng max=Alice(70000), Sales max=Charlie(55000), HR max=Frank(45000).
solution_sql: SELECT e.dept,e.name,e.salary FROM employees e INNER JOIN(SELECT dept,MAX(salary)as m FROM employees GROUP BY dept)d ON e.dept=d.dept AND e.salary=d.m ORDER BY e.salary DESC;

title: Managers and Their Team Size
problem_statement:
Find all managers and how many direct reports they have.

Return manager name and report_count, ordered by count descending.
hint: Self-join: join employees to itself on mgr_id = id.
explanation: Alice(id=1) manages Bob,Eve (2 reports). Charlie(id=3) manages Diana (1 report).
solution_sql: SELECT m.name AS manager,COUNT(e.id)AS report_count FROM employees e JOIN employees m ON e.mgr_id=m.id GROUP BY m.name ORDER BY report_count DESC;

title: Top Restaurant by Revenue
problem_statement:
Find the restaurant with the highest total revenue from orders.

Return restaurant name and total_revenue.
hint: JOIN restaurants with orders, GROUP BY restaurant, use SUM(amount).
explanation: Biryani House: 450+380+400=1230. Pizza Palace: 650+700=1350. Pizza Palace wins.
solution_sql: SELECT r.name,SUM(o.amount)AS total_revenue FROM restaurants r JOIN orders o ON r.id=o.rest_id GROUP BY r.name ORDER BY total_revenue DESC LIMIT 1;

title: Restaurants With No Orders
problem_statement:
Find restaurants that have received zero orders.

Return the restaurant name.
hint: Use LEFT JOIN and check for NULL order ids.
explanation: Dosa Corner(id=4) has order 104. All except... wait, Burger Barn has order 107. Actually all have orders. Let me check... All 5 restaurants have at least one order.
solution_sql: SELECT r.name FROM restaurants r LEFT JOIN orders o ON r.id=o.rest_id WHERE o.id IS NULL;

title: City-wise Average Rating
problem_statement:
Find the average restaurant rating for each city.

Return city and avg_rating rounded to 1 decimal, ordered by avg_rating descending.
hint: Use GROUP BY city with AVG(rating) and ROUND().
solution_sql: SELECT city,ROUND(AVG(rating),1)AS avg_rating FROM restaurants GROUP BY city ORDER BY avg_rating DESC;

title: Doctor With Most Patients
problem_statement:
Find the doctor who has treated the most unique patients.

Return doctor name and patient_count.
hint: JOIN doctors with appointments, COUNT DISTINCT patient_id.
explanation: Dr. Sharma: patients 101,103 = 2 unique. Dr. Patel: 102,103 = 2. Dr. Gupta: 104 = 1. Tie between Sharma and Patel.
solution_sql: SELECT d.name,COUNT(DISTINCT a.patient_id)AS patient_count FROM doctors d JOIN appointments a ON d.id=a.doc_id GROUP BY d.name ORDER BY patient_count DESC LIMIT 2;

title: Patients With Multiple Visits
problem_statement:
Find patients who have visited more than once.

Return patient name and visit_count.
hint: GROUP BY patient_id with HAVING COUNT>1.
explanation: Rahul(101) has 2 appointments. Amit(103) has 2 appointments.
solution_sql: SELECT p.name,COUNT(a.id)AS visit_count FROM patients p JOIN appointments a ON p.id=a.patient_id GROUP BY p.name HAVING COUNT(a.id)>1;

title: Revenue Per Specialty
problem_statement:
Calculate total revenue generated by each medical specialty.

Return specialty and total_revenue, ordered descending.
hint: JOIN doctors to appointments, SUM fees, GROUP BY specialty.
explanation: Cardiology: 500+500+300=1300. Orthopedics: 800+600=1400. Dermatology: 400.
solution_sql: SELECT d.specialty,SUM(a.fee)AS total_revenue FROM doctors d JOIN appointments a ON d.id=a.doc_id GROUP BY d.specialty ORDER BY total_revenue DESC;

title: Branch-wise Total Deposits
problem_statement:
Find total credit (deposit) amount per branch.

Return branch and total_deposits, ordered descending.
hint: JOIN accounts with transactions WHERE type='credit', GROUP BY branch.
explanation: Mumbai(acc 1,3): credits 5000+8000=13000. Delhi(acc 2): 20000. Bangalore(acc 4): 0.
solution_sql: SELECT a.branch,SUM(t.amount)AS total_deposits FROM accounts a JOIN transactions t ON a.id=t.acc_id WHERE t.type='credit' GROUP BY a.branch ORDER BY total_deposits DESC;

title: Accounts With Net Outflow
problem_statement:
Find accounts where total debits exceed total credits (net outflow).

Return holder name and net_outflow amount.
hint: Use CASE WHEN inside SUM to separate credits and debits.
explanation: Ravi: credits=5000, debits=2000+3000=5000. Net=0. Meena: credits=20000, debits=15000. Net positive. Suresh: credits=0, debits=5000. Net outflow=5000.
solution_sql: SELECT a.holder,SUM(CASE WHEN t.type='debit' THEN t.amount ELSE 0 END)-SUM(CASE WHEN t.type='credit' THEN t.amount ELSE 0 END)AS net_outflow FROM accounts a JOIN transactions t ON a.id=t.acc_id GROUP BY a.holder HAVING net_outflow>0;

title: Second Highest Salary
problem_statement:
Find the second highest distinct salary.

Return it as second_highest_salary. If none, return NULL.
hint: Use MAX with a WHERE < MAX subquery, or LIMIT/OFFSET.
solution_sql: SELECT MAX(salary)AS second_highest_salary FROM employees WHERE salary<(SELECT MAX(salary) FROM employees);

title: Repeat Customers
problem_statement:
Find customers who ordered from more than one distinct restaurant.

Return cust_id and restaurant_count, ordered descending.
hint: GROUP BY cust_id, COUNT DISTINCT rest_id, HAVING > 1.
explanation: Cust 201: restaurants 1,4,2=3. Cust 202: restaurants 2,1=2. Others: 1 each.
solution_sql: SELECT cust_id,COUNT(DISTINCT rest_id)AS restaurant_count FROM orders GROUP BY cust_id HAVING COUNT(DISTINCT rest_id)>1 ORDER BY restaurant_count DESC;

title: Employees Hired After Their Manager
problem_statement:
Find employees who were hired after their manager.

Return employee name, their hire date, and manager name.
hint: Self-join on mgr_id, compare hire_date.
explanation: Bob(2020-03-01) hired after Alice(2019-01-15)=manager. Eve(2021-08-05) after Alice. Diana(2021-01-20) after Charlie(2020-06-10).
solution_sql: SELECT e.name AS emp_name,e.hire_date,m.name AS manager_name FROM employees e JOIN employees m ON e.mgr_id=m.id WHERE e.hire_date>m.hire_date;

title: Highest Revenue Day Per Restaurant
problem_statement:
For each restaurant, find the date with the highest single-day order total.

Return restaurant name, order_date, and day_revenue.
hint: GROUP BY rest_id and order_date, then use a window function or subquery to pick the max per restaurant.
explanation: Biryani House: Jan15=450,Jan17=380,Feb10=400 → Jan15. Pizza Palace: Jan16=650,Mar5=700 → Mar5.
solution_sql: SELECT r.name,sub.order_date,sub.day_revenue FROM restaurants r JOIN(SELECT rest_id,order_date,SUM(amount)AS day_revenue FROM orders GROUP BY rest_id,order_date)sub ON r.id=sub.rest_id JOIN(SELECT rest_id,MAX(day_revenue)AS max_rev FROM(SELECT rest_id,order_date,SUM(amount)AS day_revenue FROM orders GROUP BY rest_id,order_date)GROUP BY rest_id)mx ON sub.rest_id=mx.rest_id AND sub.day_revenue=mx.max_rev;

title: Most Watched Show
problem_statement:
Find the show with the most total watch time (sum of duration_min).

Return show title and total_minutes.
hint: JOIN shows with watch_history, SUM duration, ORDER DESC LIMIT 1.
explanation: Dark: 120+45=165. Money Heist: 90+95=185. Stranger Things: 60+70=130. Sacred Games: 110. Mirzapur: 80. Money Heist wins.
solution_sql: SELECT s.title,SUM(w.duration_min)AS total_minutes FROM shows s JOIN watch_history w ON s.id=w.show_id GROUP BY s.title ORDER BY total_minutes DESC LIMIT 1;

title: Users Who Never Watched
problem_statement:
Find users who have no entries in the watch history.

Return user name.
hint: LEFT JOIN users to watch_history, WHERE watch_history.id IS NULL.
explanation: All users have at least one watch. So result is empty.
solution_sql: SELECT u.name FROM users u LEFT JOIN watch_history w ON u.id=w.user_id WHERE w.id IS NULL;

title: Genre Popularity Ranking
problem_statement:
Rank genres by total watch count (number of watch_history entries).

Return genre, watch_count, ordered descending.
hint: JOIN shows to watch_history, GROUP BY genre, COUNT(*).
explanation: Thriller: Dark(2)+Sacred Games(1)=3. Action: Money Heist(2)+Mirzapur(1)=3. Sci-Fi: Stranger Things(2)=2.
solution_sql: SELECT s.genre,COUNT(w.id)AS watch_count FROM shows s JOIN watch_history w ON s.id=w.show_id GROUP BY s.genre ORDER BY watch_count DESC;

title: Premium vs Basic Avg Watch Time
problem_statement:
Compare the average watch duration between premium and basic plan users.

Return plan and avg_duration rounded to 1 decimal.
hint: JOIN users to watch_history, GROUP BY plan, AVG(duration_min).
solution_sql: SELECT u.plan,ROUND(AVG(w.duration_min),1)AS avg_duration FROM users u JOIN watch_history w ON u.id=w.user_id GROUP BY u.plan ORDER BY avg_duration DESC;

title: Students With All A Grades
problem_statement:
Find students who received grade 'A' in ALL their enrolled courses.

Return student name.
hint: Compare COUNT of enrollments to COUNT of 'A' grades per student.
explanation: Meera: 1 course, 1 A. Others have mixed grades.
solution_sql: SELECT s.name FROM students s JOIN enrollments e ON s.id=e.student_id GROUP BY s.name HAVING COUNT(e.id)=SUM(CASE WHEN e.grade='A' THEN 1 ELSE 0 END);

title: Course Enrollment Count
problem_statement:
Find how many students are enrolled in each course.

Return course_name and student_count, ordered descending.
hint: JOIN courses with enrollments, GROUP BY course, COUNT.
explanation: Data Structures: 3 (Ravi,Arjun,Nikhil). DBMS: 2 (Ravi,Nikhil). OS: 2 (Arjun,Kavya). Signals: 1 (Meera).
solution_sql: SELECT c.course_name,COUNT(e.student_id)AS student_count FROM courses c JOIN enrollments e ON c.id=e.course_id GROUP BY c.course_name ORDER BY student_count DESC;

title: Branch Topper
problem_statement:
Find the student with the most 'A' grades in each branch.

Return branch, student name, and a_count.
hint: COUNT grade='A' per student, then pick max per branch.
explanation: CSE: Ravi has 1A, Arjun has 1A, Nikhil has 1A (tie). ECE: Meera 1A. ME: Kavya 0A.
solution_sql: SELECT s.branch,s.name,COUNT(CASE WHEN e.grade='A' THEN 1 END)AS a_count FROM students s JOIN enrollments e ON s.id=e.student_id GROUP BY s.branch,s.name HAVING a_count>0 ORDER BY s.branch,a_count DESC;

title: Department Salary Rank
problem_statement:
Rank employees within each department by salary (highest first).

Return dept, name, salary, and salary_rank. Use dense ranking.
hint: Use a subquery to count distinct higher salaries +1 as rank.
explanation: Eng: Alice=1, Eve=2, Bob=3. Sales: Charlie=1, Diana=2. HR: Frank=1.
solution_sql: SELECT e.dept,e.name,e.salary,(SELECT COUNT(DISTINCT e2.salary)+1 FROM employees e2 WHERE e2.dept=e.dept AND e2.salary>e.salary)AS salary_rank FROM employees e ORDER BY e.dept,salary_rank;

title: Shows Not Watched by Premium Users
problem_statement:
Find shows that no premium-plan user has ever watched.

Return show title.
hint: Use NOT EXISTS or NOT IN with a subquery joining users and watch_history.
explanation: Premium users (Aarav,Chirag) watched: Dark,Money Heist,Sacred Games,Mirzapur. Not watched: Stranger Things.
solution_sql: SELECT s.title FROM shows s WHERE s.id NOT IN(SELECT w.show_id FROM watch_history w JOIN users u ON w.user_id=u.id WHERE u.plan='premium');

title: Patients Older Than Average
problem_statement:
Find patients whose age is above the average patient age.

Return name and age, ordered by age descending.
hint: Subquery with AVG(age).
explanation: Avg=(35+28+45+32)/4=35. Amit(45) is above average.
solution_sql: SELECT name,age FROM patients WHERE age>(SELECT AVG(age) FROM patients) ORDER BY age DESC;

title: Salary Rank With Window Function
problem_statement:
Rank employees by salary within each department using DENSE_RANK().

Return dept, name, salary, and rnk ordered by dept then rank.
hint: Use DENSE_RANK() OVER (PARTITION BY dept ORDER BY salary DESC).
explanation: Eng: Alice=1,Eve=2,Bob=3. Sales: Charlie=1,Diana=2. HR: Frank=1.
solution_sql: SELECT dept,name,salary,DENSE_RANK() OVER(PARTITION BY dept ORDER BY salary DESC)AS rnk FROM employees ORDER BY dept,rnk;

title: Previous Order Amount (LAG)
problem_statement:
For each order, show the previous order amount for the same customer using LAG.

Return cust_id, order_date, amount, and prev_amount (NULL if first order). Order by cust_id, order_date.
hint: Use LAG(amount) OVER (PARTITION BY cust_id ORDER BY order_date).
explanation: Cust201: Jan15=450(prev NULL), Feb1=220(prev 450), Mar5=700(prev 220). Cust202: Jan16=650(NULL), Feb10=400(prev 650).
solution_sql: SELECT cust_id,order_date,amount,LAG(amount) OVER(PARTITION BY cust_id ORDER BY order_date)AS prev_amount FROM orders ORDER BY cust_id,order_date;

title: Running Total of Transactions
problem_statement:
Show a running total of transaction amounts per account, ordered by date.

Return holder, txn_date, amount, and running_total.
hint: Use SUM(amount) OVER (PARTITION BY acc_id ORDER BY txn_date).
solution_sql: SELECT a.holder,t.txn_date,t.amount,SUM(t.amount) OVER(PARTITION BY t.acc_id ORDER BY t.txn_date)AS running_total FROM accounts a JOIN transactions t ON a.id=t.acc_id ORDER BY a.holder,t.txn_date;

title: CTE: High-Value Customers
problem_statement:
Using a CTE, find customers whose total spending is above the average customer spending.

Return cust_id and total_spent, ordered descending.
hint: WITH cust_totals AS (SELECT cust_id, SUM(amount) ...) then filter WHERE total > AVG.
solution_sql: WITH cust_totals AS(SELECT cust_id,SUM(amount)AS total_spent FROM orders GROUP BY cust_id)SELECT cust_id,total_spent FROM cust_totals WHERE total_spent>(SELECT AVG(total_spent) FROM cust_totals) ORDER BY total_spent DESC;

title: UNION: Combined Activity Feed
problem_statement:
Create a unified activity log combining user registrations and watch events.

Return user_name, event_type ('Joined' or 'Watched'), and event_date. Order by event_date.
hint: UNION ALL two SELECTs: one from users (join_date) and one from watch_history joined with users.
explanation: Combine join_date entries with watch_date entries.
solution_sql: SELECT name AS user_name,'Joined' AS event_type,join_date AS event_date FROM users UNION ALL SELECT u.name,'Watched',w.watch_date FROM watch_history w JOIN users u ON w.user_id=u.id ORDER BY event_date;

title: Monthly Order Count
problem_statement:
Find the number of orders placed in each month.

Return month_num and order_count, ordered by month.
hint: Use strftime('%m', order_date) to extract month in SQLite.
solution_sql: SELECT strftime('%m',order_date)AS month_num,COUNT(*)AS order_count FROM orders GROUP BY month_num ORDER BY month_num;

title: Find Indian Cuisine Restaurants
problem_statement:
Find all restaurants whose cuisine contains the word 'Indian'.

Return name and cuisine, ordered by rating descending.
hint: Use LIKE '%Indian%'.
explanation: Dosa Corner(Indian,4.7), Biryani House(Indian,4.5).
solution_sql: SELECT name,cuisine FROM restaurants WHERE cuisine LIKE '%Indian%' ORDER BY rating DESC;

title: Handle NULL Manager
problem_statement:
List all employees with their manager name. If an employee has no manager, show 'No Manager' instead of NULL.

Return emp_name and manager_name, ordered by emp_name.
hint: Use LEFT JOIN + COALESCE(m.name, 'No Manager').
explanation: Alice,Charlie,Frank have mgr_id=NULL → 'No Manager'. Bob→Alice, Eve→Alice, Diana→Charlie.
solution_sql: SELECT e.name AS emp_name,COALESCE(m.name,'No Manager')AS manager_name FROM employees e LEFT JOIN employees m ON e.mgr_id=m.id ORDER BY e.name;

title: Three-Table Join: Patient Diagnosis by Doctor
problem_statement:
Join all three tables to show which doctor diagnosed which patient and what the diagnosis was.

Return doctor_name, patient_name, diagnosis, ordered by visit_date.
hint: JOIN doctors → appointments → patients using doc_id and patient_id.
explanation: Three-way join across doctors, appointments, and patients tables.
solution_sql: SELECT d.name AS doctor_name,p.name AS patient_name,a.diagnosis FROM appointments a JOIN doctors d ON a.doc_id=d.id JOIN patients p ON a.patient_id=p.id ORDER BY a.visit_date;

title: EXISTS: Students Enrolled in Data Structures
problem_statement:
Using EXISTS, find students who are enrolled in 'Data Structures'.

Return student name, ordered alphabetically.
hint: Use EXISTS with a correlated subquery checking enrollments + courses.
explanation: Ravi, Arjun, Nikhil are enrolled in course 101 (Data Structures).
solution_sql: SELECT s.name FROM students s WHERE EXISTS(SELECT 1 FROM enrollments e JOIN courses c ON e.course_id=c.id WHERE e.student_id=s.id AND c.course_name='Data Structures') ORDER BY s.name;

title: Next Order Date (LEAD)
problem_statement:
For each customer order, show the date of their NEXT order using LEAD.

Return customer_id, order_date, and next_order_date (NULL if last). Order by customer_id, order_date.
hint: Use LEAD(order_date) OVER (PARTITION BY customer_id ORDER BY order_date).
explanation: Ankit: Jan10→Feb05, Feb05→NULL. Priya: Jan15→Mar01, Mar01→NULL.
solution_sql: SELECT customer_id,order_date,LEAD(order_date) OVER(PARTITION BY customer_id ORDER BY order_date)AS next_order_date FROM orders ORDER BY customer_id,order_date;

title: Top Product Per Category (ROW_NUMBER)
problem_statement:
Find the most expensive product in each category using ROW_NUMBER.

Return category, product_name, and price.
hint: Use ROW_NUMBER() OVER (PARTITION BY category ORDER BY price DESC), filter rn=1.
explanation: Electronics: Laptop(55000). Fashion: Sneakers(3200). Books: Python Book(450).
solution_sql: SELECT category,product_name,price FROM(SELECT category,product_name,price,ROW_NUMBER() OVER(PARTITION BY category ORDER BY price DESC)AS rn FROM products)WHERE rn=1;

title: Gmail Users Only
problem_statement:
Find all customers whose email ends with '@gmail.com'.

Return name and email, ordered by name.
hint: Use LIKE '%@gmail.com'.
explanation: Ankit, Rohan, Vikram have gmail addresses.
solution_sql: SELECT name,email FROM customers WHERE email LIKE '%@gmail.com' ORDER BY name;

title: Orders in Date Range (BETWEEN)
problem_statement:
Find all orders placed in February 2024.

Return order id, customer_id, order_date, and total. Order by order_date.
explanation: Orders 1003(Feb05) and 1004(Feb20) fall in February.
solution_sql: SELECT id,customer_id,order_date,total FROM orders WHERE order_date BETWEEN '2024-02-01' AND '2024-02-29' ORDER BY order_date;

title: Multi-CTE: Customer Lifetime Value
problem_statement:
Using multiple CTEs, find each customer's total spending and order count (exclude cancelled orders), then rank them.

Return name, total_spent, order_count, and spending_rank.
hint: WITH order_stats AS (...), ranked AS (... DENSE_RANK() OVER ...).
solution_sql: WITH order_stats AS(SELECT customer_id,SUM(total)AS total_spent,COUNT(*)AS order_count FROM orders WHERE status!='cancelled' GROUP BY customer_id),ranked AS(SELECT os.*,DENSE_RANK() OVER(ORDER BY total_spent DESC)AS spending_rank FROM order_stats os)SELECT c.name,r.total_spent,r.order_count,r.spending_rank FROM ranked r JOIN customers c ON r.customer_id=c.id ORDER BY r.spending_rank;

title: Cross Join: All Customer-Product Pairs
problem_statement:
Generate all possible customer-product combinations.

Return customer name, product_name. Order by customer name, product_name. LIMIT 10.
hint: Use CROSS JOIN between customers and products.
explanation: 5 customers × 5 products = 25 total pairs. First 10 shown.
solution_sql: SELECT c.name,p.product_name FROM customers c CROSS JOIN products p ORDER BY c.name,p.product_name LIMIT 10;

title: EXCEPT: Products Never Ordered
problem_statement:
Find products that have never been ordered by any customer.

Return product_name.
hint: Use EXCEPT or NOT IN between products and order_items.
explanation: All products appear in order_items, so result is empty (Sneakers=104 appears in order 1006).
solution_sql: SELECT product_name FROM products WHERE id NOT IN(SELECT DISTINCT product_id FROM order_items);

title: Duplicate Email Domains
problem_statement:
Find email domains that are used by more than one customer.

Return domain and user_count, ordered descending.
hint: Use SUBSTR + INSTR to extract domain, then GROUP BY + HAVING > 1.
explanation: gmail.com: Ankit,Rohan,Vikram=3. Others have 1 each.
solution_sql: SELECT SUBSTR(email,INSTR(email,'@')+1)AS domain,COUNT(*)AS user_count FROM customers GROUP BY domain HAVING COUNT(*)>1 ORDER BY user_count DESC;

title: IFNULL: Safe Division
problem_statement:
For each account, show the average transaction amount. If an account has no transactions, show 0.

Return holder and avg_txn, ordered by holder.
hint: LEFT JOIN accounts to transactions, use IFNULL(AVG(amount), 0).
explanation: All accounts have transactions. Kiran: avg=8000. Meena: avg=17500. Ravi: avg=3333.33. Suresh: avg=5000.
solution_sql: SELECT a.holder,ROUND(IFNULL(AVG(t.amount),0),2)AS avg_txn FROM accounts a LEFT JOIN transactions t ON a.id=t.acc_id GROUP BY a.holder ORDER BY a.holder;

title: Custom Sort: Priority Status
problem_statement:
List all orders sorted by status priority: 'shipped' first, then 'delivered', then 'cancelled'.

Return order id, order_date, total, and status.
hint: Use ORDER BY CASE WHEN status='shipped' THEN 1 WHEN status='delivered' THEN 2 ELSE 3 END.
solution_sql: SELECT id,order_date,total,status FROM orders ORDER BY CASE WHEN status='shipped' THEN 1 WHEN status='delivered' THEN 2 ELSE 3 END,order_date;

title: GROUP_CONCAT: List All Followers
problem_statement:
For each user, show a comma-separated list of their followers' usernames.

Return username and followers_list, ordered by username.
hint: JOIN follows with profiles, use GROUP_CONCAT.
explanation: ankit_dev is followed by priya_codes,rohan_js,vikram_sql.
solution_sql: SELECT p.username,GROUP_CONCAT(fp.username)AS followers_list FROM profiles p JOIN follows f ON p.id=f.following_id JOIN profiles fp ON f.follower_id=fp.id GROUP BY p.username ORDER BY p.username;

title: Days Since Last Post
problem_statement:
For each user's post, calculate days since their previous post using LAG and JULIANDAY.

Return username, post_date, content, and days_gap (NULL for first). Order by username, post_date.
hint: Use LAG(post_date) OVER (...) and JULIANDAY difference.
explanation: Ankit: Jan10→NULL, Jan15→5, Mar05→50.
solution_sql: SELECT p2.username,p.post_date,p.content,CAST(JULIANDAY(p.post_date)-JULIANDAY(LAG(p.post_date) OVER(PARTITION BY p.user_id ORDER BY p.post_date))AS INT)AS days_gap FROM posts p JOIN profiles p2 ON p.user_id=p2.id ORDER BY p2.username,p.post_date;

title: 4-Table Join: Post Comments
problem_statement:
Join all four tables to show post author username, content, commenter full name, and comment.

Order by comment_date.
hint: JOIN posts→profiles (author), comments→profiles (commenter).
explanation: 4-way join across profiles, posts, comments, and profiles again.
solution_sql: SELECT author_p.username AS author,po.content,cp.full_name AS commenter,c.comment FROM comments c JOIN posts po ON c.post_id=po.id JOIN profiles author_p ON po.user_id=author_p.id JOIN profiles cp ON c.user_id=cp.id ORDER BY c.comment_date;

title: NTILE: Salary Quartiles
problem_statement:
Divide employees into salary quartiles using NTILE(4).

Return name, salary, and quartile, ordered by salary descending.
hint: Use NTILE(4) OVER (ORDER BY salary DESC).
explanation: 6 employees into 4 tiles: Q1 gets 2, Q2 gets 2, Q3 gets 1, Q4 gets 1.
solution_sql: SELECT name,salary,NTILE(4) OVER(ORDER BY salary DESC)AS quartile FROM employees ORDER BY salary DESC;

title: FIRST_VALUE: Cheapest In Category
problem_statement:
For each product, show the cheapest product name in its category using FIRST_VALUE.

Return product_name, category, price, cheapest_in_category. Order by category, price.
hint: Use FIRST_VALUE(product_name) OVER (PARTITION BY category ORDER BY price).
explanation: Electronics cheapest=Headphones. Fashion cheapest=T-Shirt. Books=Python Book.
solution_sql: SELECT product_name,category,price,FIRST_VALUE(product_name) OVER(PARTITION BY category ORDER BY price)AS cheapest_in_category FROM products ORDER BY category,price;

title: Pivot: Orders by Status
problem_statement:
Pivot report: each customer's order count by status.

Return name, delivered_count, shipped_count, cancelled_count. Order by name.
hint: Use SUM(CASE WHEN status=... THEN 1 ELSE 0 END).
explanation: Ankit: 2 delivered. Priya: 1 delivered, 1 cancelled.
solution_sql: SELECT c.name,SUM(CASE WHEN o.status='delivered' THEN 1 ELSE 0 END)AS delivered_count,SUM(CASE WHEN o.status='shipped' THEN 1 ELSE 0 END)AS shipped_count,SUM(CASE WHEN o.status='cancelled' THEN 1 ELSE 0 END)AS cancelled_count FROM customers c JOIN orders o ON c.id=o.customer_id GROUP BY c.name ORDER BY c.name;

title: Pagination: Page 2 of Posts
problem_statement:
Get the 2nd page of posts (3 per page) ordered by likes descending.

Return content and likes.
hint: Use ORDER BY likes DESC LIMIT 3 OFFSET 3.
solution_sql: SELECT content,likes FROM posts ORDER BY likes DESC LIMIT 3 OFFSET 3;

title: Find Duplicate Cities
problem_statement:
Find cities with more than one customer.

Return city and customer_count, ordered descending.
hint: GROUP BY city, HAVING COUNT > 1.
explanation: Mumbai: Ankit,Sneha = 2.
solution_sql: SELECT city,COUNT(*)AS customer_count FROM customers GROUP BY city HAVING COUNT(*)>1 ORDER BY customer_count DESC;

title: Mutual Followers
problem_statement:
Find pairs of users who mutually follow each other.

Return user_a and user_b (alphabetically), no duplicates.
hint: Self-join follows on follower↔following symmetry.
explanation: ankit_dev↔priya_codes, ankit_dev↔rohan_js are mutual.
solution_sql: SELECT p1.username AS user_a,p2.username AS user_b FROM follows f1 JOIN follows f2 ON f1.follower_id=f2.following_id AND f1.following_id=f2.follower_id JOIN profiles p1 ON f1.follower_id=p1.id JOIN profiles p2 ON f1.following_id=p2.id WHERE p1.username<p2.username ORDER BY user_a;

title: Category Revenue Percentage
problem_statement:
Calculate what percentage of total order_items revenue each category contributes.

Return category, category_revenue, pct (rounded to 1 decimal). Order by pct descending.
hint: SUM per category / total SUM * 100.
explanation: Electronics dominates with ~94.8%.
solution_sql: SELECT p.category,SUM(oi.price)AS category_revenue,ROUND(SUM(oi.price)*100.0/(SELECT SUM(price) FROM order_items),1)AS pct FROM order_items oi JOIN products p ON oi.product_id=p.id GROUP BY p.category ORDER BY pct DESC;

title: Recursive CTE: Number Sequence
problem_statement:
Using a recursive CTE, generate a sequence of numbers from 1 to 6 (one for each employee).

Return the number as n.
hint: WITH RECURSIVE seq(n) AS (SELECT 1 UNION ALL SELECT n+1 FROM seq WHERE n < 6).
explanation: Simple recursive generation: 1,2,3,4,5,6.
solution_sql: WITH RECURSIVE seq(n) AS(SELECT 1 UNION ALL SELECT n+1 FROM seq WHERE n<6)SELECT n FROM seq;

title: LAST_VALUE: Most Expensive Ride Per Rider
problem_statement:
For each ride, show the rider's most expensive ride fare using LAST_VALUE.

Return rider name, ride_date, fare, and max_fare. Order by rider name, ride_date.
hint: Use LAST_VALUE(fare) OVER (PARTITION BY rider_id ORDER BY fare ROWS BETWEEN UNBOUNDED PRECEDING AND UNBOUNDED FOLLOWING).
explanation: Aarav's max fare across all rides is 250.
solution_sql: SELECT r2.name,r.ride_date,r.fare,LAST_VALUE(r.fare) OVER(PARTITION BY r.rider_id ORDER BY r.fare ROWS BETWEEN UNBOUNDED PRECEDING AND UNBOUNDED FOLLOWING)AS max_fare FROM rides r JOIN riders r2 ON r.rider_id=r2.id ORDER BY r2.name,r.ride_date;

title: REPLACE: Clean Email Domain
problem_statement:
Replace 'gmail.com' with 'company.com' in all customer emails.

Return name and updated_email, ordered by name.
hint: Use REPLACE(email, 'gmail.com', 'company.com').
explanation: Ankit: ankit@company.com. Priya stays yahoo. Rohan: rohan@company.com.
solution_sql: SELECT name,REPLACE(email,'gmail.com','company.com')AS updated_email FROM customers ORDER BY name;

title: ABS: Fare vs Average Deviation
problem_statement:
For each completed ride, show how much the fare deviates from the average fare (absolute value).

Return ride_id, fare, and deviation rounded to 1 decimal. Order by deviation descending.
hint: Use ABS(fare - (SELECT AVG(fare) FROM rides WHERE status='completed')).
solution_sql: SELECT id,fare,ROUND(ABS(fare-(SELECT AVG(fare) FROM rides WHERE status='completed')),1)AS deviation FROM rides WHERE status='completed' ORDER BY deviation DESC;

title: Conditional COUNT: Rides by Status
problem_statement:
Count total rides, completed rides, and cancelled rides in a single query.

Return total_rides, completed, cancelled.
hint: Use COUNT(*) and SUM(CASE WHEN ...).
explanation: Total=10, Completed=9, Cancelled=1.
solution_sql: SELECT COUNT(*)AS total_rides,SUM(CASE WHEN status='completed' THEN 1 ELSE 0 END)AS completed,SUM(CASE WHEN status='cancelled' THEN 1 ELSE 0 END)AS cancelled FROM rides;

title: UPPER/LOWER: Standardize Usernames
problem_statement:
Show each profile with username in UPPER case and full_name in LOWER case.

Return upper_user and lower_name, ordered by upper_user.
hint: Use UPPER() and LOWER().
explanation: ANKIT_DEV, ankit sharma, etc.
solution_sql: SELECT UPPER(username)AS upper_user,LOWER(full_name)AS lower_name FROM profiles ORDER BY upper_user;

title: Fare Per Km: Best Value Rides
problem_statement:
Find the fare per kilometer for each completed ride.

Return rider name, pickup, dropoff, fare_per_km (rounded to 1). Order by fare_per_km ascending.
hint: fare / distance_km, JOIN with riders.
explanation: Cheapest per km first.
solution_sql: SELECT r2.name,r.pickup,r.dropoff,ROUND(r.fare/r.distance_km,1)AS fare_per_km FROM rides r JOIN riders r2 ON r.rider_id=r2.id WHERE r.status='completed' ORDER BY fare_per_km;

title: Driver Earnings Summary
problem_statement:
For each driver, show total earnings, ride count, and average fare from completed rides.

Return driver name, total_earnings, ride_count, avg_fare (rounded to 0). Order by total_earnings descending.
hint: JOIN drivers to rides, GROUP BY driver, filter completed.
explanation: Ramesh: 4 rides totaling 1070.
solution_sql: SELECT d.name,SUM(r.fare)AS total_earnings,COUNT(*)AS ride_count,ROUND(AVG(r.fare))AS avg_fare FROM drivers d JOIN rides r ON d.id=r.driver_id WHERE r.status='completed' GROUP BY d.name ORDER BY total_earnings DESC;

title: Complex WHERE: High-Value Mumbai Rides
problem_statement:
Find completed rides in Mumbai (pickup or dropoff in Andheri, Bandra, Dadar, Worli, Powai) with fare > 200.

Return ride_id, pickup, dropoff, fare. Order by fare descending.
hint: Use WHERE status='completed' AND fare > 200 AND (pickup IN (...) OR dropoff IN (...)).
explanation: Filter by multiple Mumbai locations and fare threshold.
solution_sql: SELECT id,pickup,dropoff,fare FROM rides WHERE status='completed' AND fare>200 AND(pickup IN('Andheri','Bandra','Dadar','Worli','Powai')OR dropoff IN('Andheri','Bandra','Dadar','Worli','Powai'))ORDER BY fare DESC;

title: Engagement Score: Posts + Comments
problem_statement:
Calculate an engagement score for each user: (total_likes + comment_count * 5).

Return username, total_likes, comment_count, engagement_score. Order by score descending.
hint: LEFT JOIN posts and comments to profiles, aggregate both.
explanation: ankit_dev: likes=25+42+95=162, comments received=1+3=4, score=162+20=182.
solution_sql: SELECT pr.username,IFNULL(SUM(DISTINCT p.likes),0)AS total_likes,COUNT(DISTINCT c.id)AS comments_received,IFNULL(SUM(DISTINCT p.likes),0)+COUNT(DISTINCT c.id)*5 AS engagement_score FROM profiles pr LEFT JOIN posts p ON pr.id=p.user_id LEFT JOIN comments c ON p.id=c.post_id GROUP BY pr.username ORDER BY engagement_score DESC;

title: LENGTH: Short Usernames
problem_statement:
Find profiles where the username is shorter than 10 characters.

Return username and name_length, ordered by name_length.
hint: Use LENGTH(username) < 10.
explanation: rohan_js=8, sneha_ml=8, ankit_dev=9.
solution_sql: SELECT username,LENGTH(username)AS name_length FROM profiles WHERE LENGTH(username)<10 ORDER BY name_length;

title: COUNT DISTINCT vs COUNT(*)
problem_statement:
Show the total number of orders vs the number of unique customers who ordered.

Return total_orders and unique_customers.
hint: COUNT(*) vs COUNT(DISTINCT cust_id).
explanation: 8 total orders, 5 unique customers.
solution_sql: SELECT COUNT(*)AS total_orders,COUNT(DISTINCT cust_id)AS unique_customers FROM orders;

title: IN Subquery: Doctors Who Treated Seniors
problem_statement:
Find doctors who have treated patients older than 35.

Return doctor name, ordered alphabetically.
hint: Use WHERE id IN (SELECT doc_id FROM appointments WHERE patient_id IN (...)).
explanation: Patients >35: Amit(45). Amit was treated by Dr. Sharma and Dr. Patel.
solution_sql: SELECT DISTINCT d.name FROM doctors d WHERE d.id IN(SELECT a.doc_id FROM appointments a WHERE a.patient_id IN(SELECT id FROM patients WHERE age>35))ORDER BY d.name;

title: PERCENT_RANK: Salary Percentile
problem_statement:
Calculate the percent rank of each employee's salary.

Return name, salary, and pct_rank (rounded to 2 decimal). Order by salary descending.
hint: Use PERCENT_RANK() OVER (ORDER BY salary).
solution_sql: SELECT name,salary,ROUND(PERCENT_RANK() OVER(ORDER BY salary),2)AS pct_rank FROM employees ORDER BY salary DESC;

title: HAVING Multiple Conditions
problem_statement:
Find branches where total credits exceed 10000 AND total debits exceed 2000.

Return branch, total_credits, total_debits.
hint: GROUP BY branch with two HAVING conditions using AND.
explanation: Mumbai: credits=13000, debits=5000 (both pass).
solution_sql: SELECT a.branch,SUM(CASE WHEN t.type='credit' THEN t.amount ELSE 0 END)AS total_credits,SUM(CASE WHEN t.type='debit' THEN t.amount ELSE 0 END)AS total_debits FROM accounts a JOIN transactions t ON a.id=t.acc_id GROUP BY a.branch HAVING total_credits>10000 AND total_debits>2000;

title: MIN and MAX in Same Query
problem_statement:
Find the cheapest and most expensive completed ride fare, plus the fare range.

Return min_fare, max_fare, and fare_range.
hint: Use MIN(), MAX(), and MAX()-MIN().
explanation: Min=150, Max=550, Range=400.
solution_sql: SELECT MIN(fare)AS min_fare,MAX(fare)AS max_fare,MAX(fare)-MIN(fare)AS fare_range FROM rides WHERE status='completed';

title: Nested CASE: Grade Classification
problem_statement:
Classify each enrollment into 'Excellent' (A), 'Good' (B), 'Average' (C), or 'Below Average' (other).

Return student name, course_name, grade, and classification. Order by student name.
hint: Use CASE WHEN grade='A' THEN 'Excellent' WHEN 'B' THEN 'Good' ...
explanation: Map each grade to a label.
solution_sql: SELECT s.name,c.course_name,e.grade,CASE WHEN e.grade='A' THEN 'Excellent' WHEN e.grade='B' THEN 'Good' WHEN e.grade='C' THEN 'Average' ELSE 'Below Average' END AS classification FROM enrollments e JOIN students s ON e.student_id=s.id JOIN courses c ON e.course_id=c.id ORDER BY s.name;

title: Multi-Table CTE: Top Driver Per City
problem_statement:
Using CTEs, find the top-earning driver in each city from completed rides.

Return city, driver_name, and total_earnings.
hint: WITH earnings AS (...), ranked AS (... ROW_NUMBER() ...) WHERE rn=1.
explanation: Mumbai: Ramesh(1070). Delhi: Kavita(600). Bangalore: Mohan(550). Chennai: Lakshmi(150).
solution_sql: WITH earnings AS(SELECT d.id,d.name,d.city,SUM(r.fare)AS total_earnings FROM drivers d JOIN rides r ON d.id=r.driver_id WHERE r.status='completed' GROUP BY d.id),ranked AS(SELECT *,ROW_NUMBER() OVER(PARTITION BY city ORDER BY total_earnings DESC)AS rn FROM earnings)SELECT city,name AS driver_name,total_earnings FROM ranked WHERE rn=1 ORDER BY city;

title: OR/AND Precedence: Filter Rides
problem_statement:
Find rides that are either: (completed AND fare > 300) OR (cancelled).

Return ride_id, status, fare. Order by ride_id.
hint: Parentheses matter: (status='completed' AND fare>300) OR status='cancelled'.
explanation: Completed>300: rides 3,4,6,9. Cancelled: ride 7.
solution_sql: SELECT id,status,fare FROM rides WHERE(status='completed' AND fare>300)OR status='cancelled' ORDER BY id;

title: Derived Table: Above-Avg Riders
problem_statement:
Using a derived table (inline subquery in FROM), find riders whose average fare exceeds the overall average.

Return rider name and avg_fare (rounded to 0). Order by avg_fare descending.
hint: SELECT FROM (SELECT rider_id, AVG(fare) ...) WHERE avg_fare > (overall avg).
explanation: Overall avg ≈ 291. Diya(550), Chirag(375), Bhavna(300) are above.
solution_sql: SELECT r2.name,ROUND(sub.avg_fare)AS avg_fare FROM(SELECT rider_id,AVG(fare)AS avg_fare FROM rides WHERE status='completed' GROUP BY rider_id)sub JOIN riders r2 ON sub.rider_id=r2.id WHERE sub.avg_fare>(SELECT AVG(fare) FROM rides WHERE status='completed')ORDER BY avg_fare DESC;

title: SUBSTR: Extract First Name
problem_statement:
Extract just the first name from each employee (characters before any space, but since names are single words, just return first 3 characters as initials).

Return name and initials (first 3 chars). Order by name.
hint: Use SUBSTR(name, 1, 3).
explanation: Ali, Bob, Cha, Dia, Eve, Fra.
solution_sql: SELECT name,SUBSTR(name,1,3)AS initials FROM employees ORDER BY name;

title: CAST: Fare as Integer
problem_statement:
Show each completed ride's fare cast as an integer (truncated, not rounded).

Return ride_id, original fare, and fare_int. Order by ride_id.
hint: Use CAST(fare AS INT).
solution_sql: SELECT id,fare,CAST(fare AS INT)AS fare_int FROM rides WHERE status='completed' ORDER BY id;

title: Cumulative Order Total
problem_statement:
Show each order with a running cumulative total across all orders sorted by date.

Return order_id, order_date, total, and cumulative_total. Order by order_date.
hint: Use SUM(total) OVER (ORDER BY order_date).
solution_sql: SELECT id,order_date,total,SUM(total) OVER(ORDER BY order_date)AS cumulative_total FROM orders ORDER BY order_date;

title: NOT EXISTS: Idle Drivers
problem_statement:
Find drivers who have never completed a ride using NOT EXISTS.

Return driver name.
hint: Use NOT EXISTS with a correlated subquery on rides.
explanation: All drivers have at least one completed ride, so result is empty.
solution_sql: SELECT d.name FROM drivers d WHERE NOT EXISTS(SELECT 1 FROM rides r WHERE r.driver_id=d.id AND r.status='completed');

title: Multiple UNION: All Names
problem_statement:
Combine all driver names and rider names into a single list with their role.

Return name and role ('Driver' or 'Rider'). Order by name.
hint: UNION ALL two SELECTs with literal role column.
explanation: Combined list of 10 people.
solution_sql: SELECT name,'Driver' AS role FROM drivers UNION ALL SELECT name,'Rider' FROM riders ORDER BY name;

title: Summary Row with UNION
problem_statement:
Show each branch's total balance AND a grand total row at the bottom.

Return branch and total_balance. The summary row should have branch='TOTAL'.
hint: Use UNION ALL: per-branch GROUP BY + a second SELECT for grand total.
explanation: Mumbai: 80000. Delhi: 120000. Bangalore: 75000. TOTAL: 275000.
solution_sql: SELECT branch,SUM(balance)AS total_balance FROM accounts GROUP BY branch UNION ALL SELECT 'TOTAL',SUM(balance) FROM accounts ORDER BY CASE WHEN branch='TOTAL' THEN 1 ELSE 0 END,branch;

title: Same-City Customer Pairs
problem_statement:
Find all pairs of customers who live in the same city.

Return city, customer_a, customer_b (alphabetically). No duplicate pairs.
hint: Self-join customers ON city, WHERE c1.name < c2.name.
explanation: Mumbai: Ankit & Sneha.
solution_sql: SELECT c1.city,c1.name AS customer_a,c2.name AS customer_b FROM customers c1 JOIN customers c2 ON c1.city=c2.city AND c1.name<c2.name ORDER BY c1.city;

title: RANK vs DENSE_RANK
problem_statement:
Compare RANK() and DENSE_RANK() for shows ordered by total watch time.

Return title, total_minutes, rank_val, dense_rank_val. Order by total_minutes descending.
hint: If two shows tie, RANK skips next number but DENSE_RANK doesn't.
explanation: Money Heist(185) and Dark(165) then Stranger Things(130).
solution_sql: SELECT s.title,SUM(w.duration_min)AS total_minutes,RANK() OVER(ORDER BY SUM(w.duration_min) DESC)AS rank_val,DENSE_RANK() OVER(ORDER BY SUM(w.duration_min) DESC)AS dense_rank_val FROM shows s JOIN watch_history w ON s.id=w.show_id GROUP BY s.title ORDER BY total_minutes DESC;

title: Scalar Subquery in SELECT
problem_statement:
For each restaurant, show its rating and the overall average rating using a scalar subquery.

Return name, rating, and avg_all_rating (rounded to 1). Order by rating descending.
hint: Use (SELECT ROUND(AVG(rating),1) FROM restaurants) in SELECT.
explanation: Each row shows the restaurant's rating alongside the global average.
solution_sql: SELECT name,rating,(SELECT ROUND(AVG(rating),1) FROM restaurants)AS avg_all_rating FROM restaurants ORDER BY rating DESC;

title: Complex Date Filter: Q1 High-Value
problem_statement:
Find delivered orders from Q1 2024 (Jan-Mar) with total > 1000.

Return order_id, order_date, total. Order by total descending.
hint: WHERE status='delivered' AND order_date BETWEEN ... AND total > 1000.
explanation: Orders 1001(57500) and 1006(6900) qualify.
solution_sql: SELECT id,order_date,total FROM orders WHERE status='delivered' AND order_date BETWEEN '2024-01-01' AND '2024-03-31' AND total>1000 ORDER BY total DESC;

title: Moving Average: 2-Ride Window
problem_statement:
For each rider, calculate a 2-ride moving average of fare (current + previous ride).

Return rider name, ride_date, fare, and moving_avg (rounded to 0). Order by rider name, ride_date.
hint: Use AVG(fare) OVER (PARTITION BY rider_id ORDER BY ride_date ROWS BETWEEN 1 PRECEDING AND CURRENT ROW).
solution_sql: SELECT r2.name,r.ride_date,r.fare,ROUND(AVG(r.fare) OVER(PARTITION BY r.rider_id ORDER BY r.ride_date ROWS BETWEEN 1 PRECEDING AND CURRENT ROW))AS moving_avg FROM rides r JOIN riders r2 ON r.rider_id=r2.id ORDER BY r2.name,r.ride_date;

title: COALESCE: Multi-Fallback
problem_statement:
Show each patient's diagnosis. If NULL, show 'Under Observation'.

Return patient name, diagnosis_display. Order by name.
hint: Use COALESCE(diagnosis, 'Under Observation').
explanation: All patients in this dataset have diagnoses, so COALESCE acts as a safety net.
solution_sql: SELECT name,COALESCE(diagnosis,'Under Observation')AS diagnosis_display FROM patients ORDER BY name;

title: Nested 3-Level Subquery
problem_statement:
Find student names who got grade 'A' in courses taught in the 'CSE' department.

Return student name, ordered alphabetically.
hint: 3-level nesting: students → enrollments → courses WHERE dept='CSE'.
explanation: CSE courses (101,103). Grade A in CSE: Ravi(101), Nikhil(103), Arjun(101).
solution_sql: SELECT s.name FROM students s WHERE s.id IN(SELECT e.student_id FROM enrollments e WHERE e.grade='A' AND e.course_id IN(SELECT c.id FROM courses c WHERE c.department='CSE'))ORDER BY s.name;

title: Anti-Join: Riders Without Rides
problem_statement:
Using LEFT JOIN + WHERE NULL pattern, find riders who have no completed rides.

Return rider name.
hint: LEFT JOIN riders to rides WHERE rides.id IS NULL.
explanation: All riders have at least one ride, so result is empty.
solution_sql: SELECT r2.name FROM riders r2 LEFT JOIN rides r ON r2.id=r.rider_id AND r.status='completed' WHERE r.id IS NULL;

title: GROUP BY Expression: Order Month
problem_statement:
Group orders by month name and show total revenue per month.

Return month_name and monthly_revenue. Order by revenue descending.
hint: Use strftime('%m', order_date) to extract month, CASE to name it.
solution_sql: SELECT CASE strftime('%m',order_date) WHEN '01' THEN 'January' WHEN '02' THEN 'February' WHEN '03' THEN 'March' END AS month_name,SUM(total)AS monthly_revenue FROM orders GROUP BY strftime('%m',order_date) ORDER BY monthly_revenue DESC;

title: Running Percentage of Total
problem_statement:
Show each post's likes as a running percentage of total likes across all posts.

Return content, likes, and running_pct (rounded to 1). Order by likes descending.
hint: SUM(likes) OVER (ORDER BY likes DESC) / total * 100.
explanation: Deployed to production!(95)=21.9%, then cumulative grows...
solution_sql: SELECT content,likes,ROUND(SUM(likes) OVER(ORDER BY likes DESC)*100.0/(SELECT SUM(likes) FROM posts),1)AS running_pct FROM posts ORDER BY likes DESC;

title: Multiple GROUP BY Columns
problem_statement:
Count rides grouped by both driver city and ride status.

Return city, status, and ride_count. Order by city, status.
hint: GROUP BY d.city, r.status.
explanation: Breakdown per city per status.
solution_sql: SELECT d.city,r.status,COUNT(*)AS ride_count FROM rides r JOIN drivers d ON r.driver_id=d.id GROUP BY d.city,r.status ORDER BY d.city,r.status;

title: CASE in WHERE Clause
problem_statement:
Find restaurants that are considered 'premium' (rating >= 4.5) OR have the cuisine 'Chinese'.

Return name, cuisine, rating. Order by rating descending.
hint: WHERE (rating >= 4.5) OR (cuisine = 'Chinese').
explanation: Dosa Corner(4.7), Biryani House(4.5), Dragon Wok(Chinese,4.2).
solution_sql: SELECT name,cuisine,rating FROM restaurants WHERE rating>=4.5 OR cuisine='Chinese' ORDER BY rating DESC;

title: Simulated UPDATE: Salary Hike
problem_statement:
Show what each employee's salary would be after a 10% hike for Engineering dept and 5% for others.

Return name, department, current_salary, new_salary. Order by new_salary descending.
hint: Use CASE WHEN department='Engineering' THEN salary*1.10 ELSE salary*1.05.
explanation: Engineering gets 10% bump, others 5%.
solution_sql: SELECT name,department,salary AS current_salary,CASE WHEN department='Engineering' THEN ROUND(salary*1.10) ELSE ROUND(salary*1.05) END AS new_salary FROM employees ORDER BY new_salary DESC;

title: CTE + Window Combo: Ranked Monthly Revenue
problem_statement:
Using a CTE to calculate monthly revenue, then rank months by revenue.

Return month_num, monthly_revenue, and revenue_rank.
hint: WITH monthly AS (...GROUP BY month), SELECT with DENSE_RANK().
explanation: January is #1, February #2, March #3.
solution_sql: WITH monthly AS(SELECT strftime('%m',order_date)AS month_num,SUM(total)AS monthly_revenue FROM orders GROUP BY strftime('%m',order_date))SELECT month_num,monthly_revenue,DENSE_RANK() OVER(ORDER BY monthly_revenue DESC)AS revenue_rank FROM monthly;

title: Full Analytics: Driver Scorecard
problem_statement:
Build a driver scorecard: for each driver show name, city, total completed rides, total earnings, average fare (rounded), their city rank by earnings, and whether they are 'Top Earner' (rank 1) or 'Regular'.

Return name, city, rides, earnings, avg_fare, city_rank, badge. Order by city, city_rank.
hint: CTE for stats, DENSE_RANK per city, CASE for badge.
explanation: Ramesh is top in Mumbai, Kavita top in Delhi, etc.
solution_sql: WITH stats AS(SELECT d.name,d.city,COUNT(*)AS rides,SUM(r.fare)AS earnings,ROUND(AVG(r.fare))AS avg_fare FROM drivers d JOIN rides r ON d.id=r.driver_id WHERE r.status='completed' GROUP BY d.id),ranked AS(SELECT *,DENSE_RANK() OVER(PARTITION BY city ORDER BY earnings DESC)AS city_rank FROM stats)SELECT name,city,rides,earnings,avg_fare,city_rank,CASE WHEN city_rank=1 THEN 'Top Earner' ELSE 'Regular' END AS badge FROM ranked ORDER BY city,city_rank;

title: Repeat Buyers: Customer Cohort
problem_statement:
Classify customers into cohorts: 'One-Timer' (1 order), 'Repeat' (2-3 orders), 'Loyal' (4+ orders). Exclude cancelled.

Return cohort and customer_count. Order by customer_count descending.
hint: CTE counts orders per customer, CASE classifies, outer GROUP BY.
explanation: One-Timer: Rohan,Sneha,Vikram=3. Repeat: Ankit(2),Priya(1 non-cancelled)=2. Loyal: 0.
solution_sql: WITH order_counts AS(SELECT customer_id,COUNT(*)AS cnt FROM orders WHERE status!='cancelled' GROUP BY customer_id),cohorts AS(SELECT CASE WHEN cnt>=4 THEN 'Loyal' WHEN cnt>=2 THEN 'Repeat' ELSE 'One-Timer' END AS cohort FROM order_counts)SELECT cohort,COUNT(*)AS customer_count FROM cohorts GROUP BY cohort ORDER BY customer_count DESC;

title: Top-2 Posts Per User (With Ties)
problem_statement:
Find each user's top 2 most-liked posts, keeping ties.

Return username, content, likes, and post_rank. Order by username, post_rank.
hint: Use DENSE_RANK per user by likes DESC, filter <=2.
explanation: ankit_dev: Deployed(95,rank1), SQL is amazing(42,rank2). priya_codes: Open source(88,rank1), Python vs JS(56,rank2).
solution_sql: SELECT username,content,likes,post_rank FROM(SELECT pr.username,p.content,p.likes,DENSE_RANK() OVER(PARTITION BY p.user_id ORDER BY p.likes DESC)AS post_rank FROM posts p JOIN profiles pr ON p.user_id=pr.id)WHERE post_rank<=2 ORDER BY username,post_rank;

title: Riders Who Are Also Drivers (Name Match)
problem_statement:
Find people whose name appears in BOTH the riders table AND the drivers table.

Return name.
hint: Use INTERSECT or IN subquery to find overlapping names.
explanation: No names overlap between riders and drivers.
solution_sql: SELECT r.name FROM riders r WHERE r.name IN(SELECT d.name FROM drivers d);

title: Grand Pipeline: Complete E-Commerce Report
problem_statement:
Build a complete business report using a multi-CTE pipeline:
1. Calculate per-customer stats (orders, spending)
2. Rank customers by spending
3. Add their most-bought category

Return name, total_orders, total_spent, spending_rank, top_category. Order by spending_rank.
hint: 3 CTEs chained: customer_stats → ranked → top_categories, final JOIN.
explanation: Ankit: 2 orders, 58299 spent, rank 1, top category Electronics.
solution_sql: WITH customer_stats AS(SELECT c.id,c.name,COUNT(*)AS total_orders,SUM(o.total)AS total_spent FROM customers c JOIN orders o ON c.id=o.customer_id WHERE o.status!='cancelled' GROUP BY c.id),ranked AS(SELECT *,DENSE_RANK() OVER(ORDER BY total_spent DESC)AS spending_rank FROM customer_stats),top_cat AS(SELECT o.customer_id,p.category,SUM(oi.qty)AS cat_qty,ROW_NUMBER() OVER(PARTITION BY o.customer_id ORDER BY SUM(oi.qty) DESC)AS rn FROM orders o JOIN order_items oi ON o.id=oi.order_id JOIN products p ON oi.product_id=p.id WHERE o.status!='cancelled' GROUP BY o.customer_id,p.category)SELECT r.name,r.total_orders,r.total_spent,r.spending_rank,tc.category AS top_category FROM ranked r LEFT JOIN top_cat tc ON r.id=tc.customer_id AND tc.rn=1 ORDER BY r.spending_rank;

title: Nth Highest Salary (N=3)
problem_statement:
Find the 3rd highest salary from the employees table. If fewer than 3 distinct salaries exist, return NULL.

Return third_highest_salary.
hint: Use DENSE_RANK or LIMIT/OFFSET with DISTINCT.
explanation: Salaries: 70K,65K,60K,55K,50K,45K. 3rd highest = 60000.
solution_sql: SELECT DISTINCT salary AS third_highest_salary FROM employees ORDER BY salary DESC LIMIT 1 OFFSET 2;

title: Second Highest Salary (LC #176)
problem_statement:
Find the second highest distinct salary. Return NULL if only one distinct salary exists.

Return second_highest.
hint: Use DENSE_RANK() or LIMIT 1 OFFSET 1.
explanation: Second highest = 65000 (Eve).
solution_sql: SELECT MAX(salary)AS second_highest FROM employees WHERE salary<(SELECT MAX(salary) FROM employees);

title: Employee Earns More Than Manager (LC #181)
problem_statement:
Given that employees have a manager_id, find employees who earn more than their manager. Since our schema doesn't have manager_id, use departments: find employees who earn more than the average of their department.

Return name, salary, department, dept_avg (rounded). Order by salary descending.
hint: Self-referencing comparison: salary vs department average.
explanation: Alice(70K) vs Engineering avg(63333)=above. Eve(65K)=above. Bob(60K) vs Marketing avg(55K)=above.
solution_sql: SELECT e.name,e.salary,e.department,ROUND(dept.avg_sal)AS dept_avg FROM employees e JOIN(SELECT department,AVG(salary)AS avg_sal FROM employees GROUP BY department)dept ON e.department=dept.department WHERE e.salary>dept.avg_sal ORDER BY e.salary DESC;

title: Restaurants With No Orders (LC #183 Pattern)
problem_statement:
Find restaurants that have never received an order.

Return restaurant name.
hint: LEFT JOIN restaurants to orders WHERE order is NULL, or NOT EXISTS.
explanation: All restaurants have orders except... let's check.
solution_sql: SELECT r.name FROM restaurants r LEFT JOIN orders o ON r.id=o.rest_id WHERE o.id IS NULL;

title: Consecutive Numbers (LC #180)
problem_statement:
Find users who posted on at least 3 consecutive days.

Return distinct username.
hint: Use LAG/LEAD to check consecutive dates, or row_number gap technique.
explanation: Aarav: Jan10,11,12(3 consecutive✓). Chirag: not posting on consecutive days.
solution_sql: WITH dated AS(SELECT user_id,login_date,JULIANDAY(login_date)-ROW_NUMBER() OVER(PARTITION BY user_id ORDER BY login_date)AS grp FROM(SELECT DISTINCT user_id,login_date FROM logins)),streaks AS(SELECT user_id,grp,COUNT(*)AS streak FROM dated GROUP BY user_id,grp HAVING COUNT(*)>=3)SELECT DISTINCT u.name FROM streaks s JOIN users u ON s.user_id=u.id ORDER BY u.name;

title: Month-over-Month Revenue Growth
problem_statement:
Calculate MoM revenue growth percentage.

Return month_num, revenue, prev_month_revenue, and growth_pct (rounded to 1). Order by month_num.
hint: Use LAG(revenue) OVER (ORDER BY month) to get previous month, then (current-prev)/prev*100.
solution_sql: WITH monthly AS(SELECT strftime('%m',order_date)AS month_num,SUM(total)AS revenue FROM orders GROUP BY strftime('%m',order_date))SELECT month_num,revenue,LAG(revenue) OVER(ORDER BY month_num)AS prev_month_revenue,ROUND((revenue-LAG(revenue) OVER(ORDER BY month_num))*100.0/LAG(revenue) OVER(ORDER BY month_num),1)AS growth_pct FROM monthly ORDER BY month_num;

title: Exchange Seat IDs (LC #626)
problem_statement:
Swap adjacent student seat IDs: student in seat 1↔2, 3↔4, etc. If odd total, last student stays.

Return student id (swapped) and name. Order by new id.
hint: Use CASE: if id is odd and not last, id+1. If even, id-1. If odd and last, keep id.
explanation: Swap adjacent rows using modular arithmetic.
solution_sql: SELECT CASE WHEN id%2=1 AND id=(SELECT MAX(id) FROM students) THEN id WHEN id%2=1 THEN id+1 ELSE id-1 END AS new_id,name FROM students ORDER BY new_id;

title: Department Top 3 Salaries (LC #185)
problem_statement:
Find the top 3 earners in each department. Include ties.

Return department, name, salary. Order by department, salary descending.
hint: DENSE_RANK() OVER (PARTITION BY department ORDER BY salary DESC) <= 3.
explanation: Engineering: Alice(70K), Eve(65K), Charlie(55K). Marketing: Bob(60K), Diana(50K). Sales: Frank(45K).
solution_sql: SELECT department,name,salary FROM(SELECT department,name,salary,DENSE_RANK() OVER(PARTITION BY department ORDER BY salary DESC)AS rn FROM employees)WHERE rn<=3 ORDER BY department,salary DESC;

title: Delete Duplicate Emails (Keep Lowest ID)
problem_statement:
Show which employee records to KEEP if we had duplicates — keep the row with the lowest ID per department.

Return the employee id and name that would be kept (first employee per department). Order by id.
hint: ROW_NUMBER() OVER (PARTITION BY department ORDER BY id) = 1.
explanation: Engineering first=Alice(1). Marketing first=Bob(2). Sales first=Frank(6).
solution_sql: SELECT id,name,department FROM(SELECT id,name,department,ROW_NUMBER() OVER(PARTITION BY department ORDER BY id)AS rn FROM employees)WHERE rn=1 ORDER BY id;

title: DAU over MAU Ratio
problem_statement:
Calculate the Daily Active Users to Monthly Active Users ratio for January 2024. DAU = average distinct daily users. MAU = total distinct users in the month.

Return dau_avg (rounded to 1), mau, and dau_mau_ratio (rounded to 2).
hint: DAU = COUNT(DISTINCT user_id) per day averaged. MAU = COUNT(DISTINCT user_id) for the month.
explanation: MAU=5. Daily counts vary. DAU avg = total daily uniques / num days.
solution_sql: WITH daily AS(SELECT login_date,COUNT(DISTINCT user_id)AS dau FROM logins WHERE login_date BETWEEN '2024-01-01' AND '2024-01-31' GROUP BY login_date),monthly AS(SELECT COUNT(DISTINCT user_id)AS mau FROM logins WHERE login_date BETWEEN '2024-01-01' AND '2024-01-31')SELECT ROUND(AVG(d.dau),1)AS dau_avg,m.mau,ROUND(AVG(d.dau)*1.0/m.mau,2)AS dau_mau_ratio FROM daily d,monthly m;

title: Funnel: Cart to Purchase
problem_statement:
Simulate a conversion funnel. From the orders table, count orders by status to show: total_orders, delivered (purchased), shipped (in progress), cancelled (dropped).

Return stage, count, and conversion_pct (relative to total, rounded to 1).
hint: Use COUNT(*) for total, then CASE WHEN for each stage.
explanation: Total=7. Delivered=5(71.4%). Shipped=1(14.3%). Cancelled=1(14.3%).
solution_sql: SELECT 'Total' AS stage,COUNT(*)AS cnt,100.0 AS conversion_pct FROM orders UNION ALL SELECT status,COUNT(*),ROUND(COUNT(*)*100.0/(SELECT COUNT(*) FROM orders),1) FROM orders GROUP BY status ORDER BY cnt DESC;

title: Customer Churn: No Order in 30+ Days
problem_statement:
Find customers whose last order was more than 30 days before the most recent order date in the system (2024-03-15).

Return name, last_order_date, days_since. Order by days_since descending.
hint: MAX(order_date) per customer, then JULIANDAY diff from system max.
explanation: Priya last ordered Feb 05 = 39 days. Diya never ordered via this schema.
solution_sql: WITH last_orders AS(SELECT c.name,MAX(o.order_date)AS last_order_date FROM customers c JOIN orders o ON c.id=o.customer_id GROUP BY c.id),max_date AS(SELECT MAX(order_date)AS md FROM orders)SELECT lo.name,lo.last_order_date,CAST(JULIANDAY(mx.md)-JULIANDAY(lo.last_order_date) AS INT)AS days_since FROM last_orders lo,max_date mx WHERE JULIANDAY(mx.md)-JULIANDAY(lo.last_order_date)>30 ORDER BY days_since DESC;

title: User's First Transaction
problem_statement:
Find each user's first successful payment.

Return user name, first_pay_date, amount, method. Order by first_pay_date.
hint: ROW_NUMBER() OVER (PARTITION BY user_id ORDER BY pay_date) = 1, filter success.
explanation: Aarav's first success: Jan 10, 500, UPI.
solution_sql: SELECT u.name,p.pay_date AS first_pay_date,p.amount,p.method FROM(SELECT *,ROW_NUMBER() OVER(PARTITION BY user_id ORDER BY pay_date)AS rn FROM payments WHERE status='success')p JOIN pay_users u ON p.user_id=u.id WHERE p.rn=1 ORDER BY first_pay_date;

title: Failed Transactions by Payment Method
problem_statement:
Count total and failed transactions per payment method. Calculate failure rate.

Return method, total_txns, failed_txns, failure_rate_pct (rounded to 1). Order by failure_rate descending.
hint: GROUP BY method, conditional COUNT for failures.
explanation: UPI: 6 total, 1 failed = 16.7%. Card: 5 total, 1 failed = 20%. Wallet: 3 total, 1 failed = 33.3%.
solution_sql: SELECT method,COUNT(*)AS total_txns,SUM(CASE WHEN status='failed' THEN 1 ELSE 0 END)AS failed_txns,ROUND(SUM(CASE WHEN status='failed' THEN 1 ELSE 0 END)*100.0/COUNT(*),1)AS failure_rate_pct FROM payments GROUP BY method ORDER BY failure_rate_pct DESC;

title: Peak Order Hours
problem_statement:
Find which hour of the day has the most orders. Since our orders use dates not times, find the day with the most orders.

Return order_date and order_count. Show only the busiest day.
hint: GROUP BY date, ORDER BY count DESC, LIMIT 1.
explanation: Multiple orders on the busiest day.
solution_sql: SELECT order_date,COUNT(*)AS order_count FROM orders GROUP BY order_date ORDER BY order_count DESC LIMIT 1;

title: Rolling 3-Payment Average
problem_statement:
For each successful payment by Aarav (user_id=1), show a rolling average of the last 3 payments.

Return pay_date, amount, and rolling_avg (rounded to 0). Order by pay_date.
hint: AVG(amount) OVER (ORDER BY pay_date ROWS BETWEEN 2 PRECEDING AND CURRENT ROW).
solution_sql: SELECT pay_date,amount,ROUND(AVG(amount) OVER(ORDER BY pay_date ROWS BETWEEN 2 PRECEDING AND CURRENT ROW))AS rolling_avg FROM payments WHERE user_id=1 AND status='success' ORDER BY pay_date;

title: Longest Consecutive Login Streak
problem_statement:
Find the longest consecutive login streak for each user.

Return user name and max_streak. Order by max_streak descending.
hint: Gaps & Islands: date - ROW_NUMBER() groups consecutive dates. Then MAX(COUNT) per user.
explanation: Chirag: 5 consecutive days (Jan 10-14). Eshan: 4 days. Aarav: 3 days.
solution_sql: WITH dated AS(SELECT user_id,login_date,JULIANDAY(login_date)-ROW_NUMBER() OVER(PARTITION BY user_id ORDER BY login_date)AS grp FROM(SELECT DISTINCT user_id,login_date FROM logins)),streaks AS(SELECT user_id,COUNT(*)AS streak FROM dated GROUP BY user_id,grp)SELECT u.name,MAX(s.streak)AS max_streak FROM streaks s JOIN users u ON s.user_id=u.id GROUP BY u.name ORDER BY max_streak DESC;

title: Friend Request Acceptance Rate
problem_statement:
From the follows table, calculate the mutual follow (acceptance) rate: what percentage of follows are reciprocated?

Return total_follows, mutual_follows, acceptance_rate_pct (rounded to 1).
hint: Self-join follows to find reciprocated pairs, then divide by total.
explanation: Total follows=9. Mutual pairs: (1↔2),(1↔3),(2↔1),(3↔1),(2↔3 not mutual). Count reciprocated follow rows.
solution_sql: WITH mutual AS(SELECT f1.id FROM follows f1 WHERE EXISTS(SELECT 1 FROM follows f2 WHERE f2.follower_id=f1.following_id AND f2.following_id=f1.follower_id))SELECT(SELECT COUNT(*) FROM follows)AS total_follows,COUNT(*)AS mutual_follows,ROUND(COUNT(*)*100.0/(SELECT COUNT(*) FROM follows),1)AS acceptance_rate_pct FROM mutual;

title: Credit Score Bucketing
problem_statement:
Classify users by their total successful spending into credit buckets: 'Platinum' (>3000), 'Gold' (1000-3000), 'Silver' (<1000).

Return name, total_spent, and tier. Order by total_spent descending.
hint: SUM with CASE WHEN for tier classification.
explanation: Diya: 5000=Platinum. Chirag: 2600=Gold. Aarav: 3300=Platinum. etc.
solution_sql: SELECT u.name,SUM(p.amount)AS total_spent,CASE WHEN SUM(p.amount)>3000 THEN 'Platinum' WHEN SUM(p.amount)>=1000 THEN 'Gold' ELSE 'Silver' END AS tier FROM pay_users u JOIN payments p ON u.id=p.user_id WHERE p.status='success' GROUP BY u.name ORDER BY total_spent DESC;

title: Surge Pricing: Rides Above 2x Average
problem_statement:
Identify completed rides where the fare exceeded twice the overall average fare (surge-priced).

Return ride_id, rider name, fare, avg_fare (rounded), and surge_multiple (rounded to 1). Order by fare descending.
hint: Scalar subquery for average, then fare > 2 * avg.
explanation: Avg completed fare ≈ 291. 2x = 582. Only ride #6 (550) is close but below. Actually 550 < 582.
solution_sql: SELECT r.id,r2.name,r.fare,ROUND((SELECT AVG(fare) FROM rides WHERE status='completed'))AS avg_fare,ROUND(r.fare/(SELECT AVG(fare) FROM rides WHERE status='completed'),1)AS surge_multiple FROM rides r JOIN riders r2 ON r.rider_id=r2.id WHERE r.status='completed' AND r.fare>2*(SELECT AVG(fare) FROM rides WHERE status='completed')ORDER BY r.fare DESC;

title: Best-Selling Product Per Month
problem_statement:
Find the best-selling product (by quantity) for each month.

Return month_num, product_name, total_qty. Order by month_num.
hint: CTE: SUM qty per product per month, ROW_NUMBER per month partition.
explanation: Jan: which product sold most? Feb? Mar?
solution_sql: WITH monthly_sales AS(SELECT strftime('%m',o.order_date)AS month_num,p.product_name,SUM(oi.qty)AS total_qty FROM orders o JOIN order_items oi ON o.id=oi.order_id JOIN products p ON oi.product_id=p.id GROUP BY strftime('%m',o.order_date),p.product_name),ranked AS(SELECT *,ROW_NUMBER() OVER(PARTITION BY month_num ORDER BY total_qty DESC)AS rn FROM monthly_sales)SELECT month_num,product_name,total_qty FROM ranked WHERE rn=1 ORDER BY month_num;

title: Items Frequently Bought Together
problem_statement:
Find pairs of products that appear in the same order. Show each unique pair once.

Return product_a, product_b, times_together. Order by times_together descending.
hint: Self-join order_items on order_id where product_id differs, deduplicate pairs.
explanation: Which products co-occur in orders?
solution_sql: SELECT p1.product_name AS product_a,p2.product_name AS product_b,COUNT(*)AS times_together FROM order_items oi1 JOIN order_items oi2 ON oi1.order_id=oi2.order_id AND oi1.product_id<oi2.product_id JOIN products p1 ON oi1.product_id=p1.id JOIN products p2 ON oi2.product_id=p2.id GROUP BY p1.product_name,p2.product_name ORDER BY times_together DESC;

title: Restaurant With Highest Average Order
problem_statement:
Find the restaurant with the highest average order amount.

Return restaurant name, avg_order (rounded to 0), and total_orders.
hint: JOIN restaurants to orders, GROUP BY, ORDER BY AVG DESC LIMIT 1.
explanation: Which restaurant has the richest orders on average?
solution_sql: SELECT r.name,ROUND(AVG(o.amount))AS avg_order,COUNT(*)AS total_orders FROM restaurants r JOIN orders o ON r.id=o.rest_id GROUP BY r.name ORDER BY avg_order DESC LIMIT 1;

title: Cuisine-Wise Revenue Share
problem_statement:
Calculate what percentage of total revenue each cuisine type generates.

Return cuisine, total_revenue, and pct (rounded to 1). Order by pct descending.
hint: SUM per cuisine / grand total * 100.
explanation: Indian, Chinese, etc. revenue shares.
solution_sql: SELECT r.cuisine,SUM(o.amount)AS total_revenue,ROUND(SUM(o.amount)*100.0/(SELECT SUM(amount) FROM orders),1)AS pct FROM restaurants r JOIN orders o ON r.id=o.rest_id GROUP BY r.cuisine ORDER BY pct DESC;

title: Simulate Return Rate by Category
problem_statement:
Treat 'cancelled' orders as returns. Calculate the return rate per product category.

Return category, total_orders, cancelled_orders, return_rate_pct (rounded to 1). Order by return_rate descending.
hint: JOIN orders→order_items→products. Conditional count of cancelled.
explanation: Which category has the most cancellations?
solution_sql: SELECT p.category,COUNT(*)AS total_orders,SUM(CASE WHEN o.status='cancelled' THEN 1 ELSE 0 END)AS cancelled_orders,ROUND(SUM(CASE WHEN o.status='cancelled' THEN 1 ELSE 0 END)*100.0/COUNT(*),1)AS return_rate_pct FROM orders o JOIN order_items oi ON o.id=oi.order_id JOIN products p ON oi.product_id=p.id GROUP BY p.category ORDER BY return_rate_pct DESC;

title: Daily Active Transacting Users
problem_statement:
Count the number of distinct users who made a successful payment on each day.

Return pay_date and active_users. Order by pay_date.
hint: COUNT(DISTINCT user_id) WHERE status='success' GROUP BY pay_date.
explanation: Jan 10: Aarav. Jan 13: Bhavna. Jan 15: Aarav. etc.
solution_sql: SELECT pay_date,COUNT(DISTINCT user_id)AS active_users FROM payments WHERE status='success' GROUP BY pay_date ORDER BY pay_date;

title: Driver Utilization: Rides Per Driver Per Month
problem_statement:
Calculate each driver's ride count per month and their utilization rate compared to the busiest driver that month.

Return month, driver_name, rides, max_rides_that_month, utilization_pct (rounded). Order by month, rides descending.
hint: CTE for monthly rides, window MAX for max per month.
explanation: Ramesh dominates in January.
solution_sql: WITH monthly AS(SELECT strftime('%m',r.ride_date)AS month,d.name AS driver_name,COUNT(*)AS rides FROM rides r JOIN drivers d ON r.driver_id=d.id GROUP BY strftime('%m',r.ride_date),d.name)SELECT month,driver_name,rides,MAX(rides) OVER(PARTITION BY month)AS max_rides,ROUND(rides*100/MAX(rides) OVER(PARTITION BY month))AS utilization_pct FROM monthly ORDER BY month,rides DESC;

title: SLA Breach: Late Appointments
problem_statement:
Find appointments where the doctor saw the patient more than 2 days after the appointment date (simulated SLA breach). Since we only have appointment_date, find appointments where the date is before '2024-01-10' as overdue.

Return patient name, doctor name, date. Order by date.
hint: Simple date filter as SLA simulation.
explanation: Early appointments that could be 'overdue'.
solution_sql: SELECT p.name AS patient,d.name AS doctor,a.appointment_date FROM appointments a JOIN patients p ON a.patient_id=p.id JOIN doctors d ON a.doc_id=d.id WHERE a.appointment_date<'2024-01-10' ORDER BY a.appointment_date;

title: Salary Above Department Average (Correlated)
problem_statement:
Using a correlated subquery, find employees whose salary is above their department's average.

Return name, department, salary. Order by department, salary descending.
hint: WHERE salary > (SELECT AVG(salary) FROM employees e2 WHERE e2.department = e.department).
explanation: Uses correlated subquery instead of JOIN.
solution_sql: SELECT name,department,salary FROM employees e WHERE salary>(SELECT AVG(salary) FROM employees e2 WHERE e2.department=e.department)ORDER BY department,salary DESC;

title: Fiscal Quarter Revenue Report
problem_statement:
Generate a quarterly revenue report. Map months to fiscal quarters: Q1(Jan-Mar), Q2(Apr-Jun), Q3(Jul-Sep), Q4(Oct-Dec).

Return quarter, order_count, and total_revenue. Order by quarter.
hint: CASE on strftime('%m') to assign quarter, GROUP BY quarter.
explanation: All our orders are in Q1 2024.
solution_sql: SELECT CASE WHEN CAST(strftime('%m',order_date)AS INT) BETWEEN 1 AND 3 THEN 'Q1' WHEN CAST(strftime('%m',order_date)AS INT) BETWEEN 4 AND 6 THEN 'Q2' WHEN CAST(strftime('%m',order_date)AS INT) BETWEEN 7 AND 9 THEN 'Q3' ELSE 'Q4' END AS quarter,COUNT(*)AS order_count,SUM(total)AS total_revenue FROM orders GROUP BY quarter ORDER BY quarter;

title: Employees Starting With 'A'
problem_statement:
Find all employees whose name starts with the letter 'A'.

Return name and department. Order by name.
hint: Use LIKE 'A%'.
explanation: Alice is the only one starting with A.
solution_sql: SELECT name,department FROM employees WHERE name LIKE 'A%' ORDER BY name;

title: Employees With Same Salary
problem_statement:
Find pairs of employees who have the same salary.

Return emp_a, emp_b, salary. No duplicate pairs. Order by salary descending.
hint: Self-join WHERE e1.salary = e2.salary AND e1.name < e2.name.
explanation: No employees share a salary in our dataset, so result is empty.
solution_sql: SELECT e1.name AS emp_a,e2.name AS emp_b,e1.salary FROM employees e1 JOIN employees e2 ON e1.salary=e2.salary AND e1.name<e2.name ORDER BY e1.salary DESC;

title: Department-Wise Employee Count
problem_statement:
Count the number of employees in each department.

Return department and emp_count. Order by emp_count descending.
hint: Simple GROUP BY department with COUNT.
explanation: Engineering: 3. Marketing: 2. Sales: 1.
solution_sql: SELECT department,COUNT(*)AS emp_count FROM employees GROUP BY department ORDER BY emp_count DESC;

title: Lead Conversion: Order Status Pipeline
problem_statement:
Create a sales pipeline view: show how many orders are at each stage, and what percentage each stage is of total.

Return status, count, pct (rounded to 1). Order by count descending.
hint: GROUP BY status, divide by total COUNT.
explanation: delivered=5(71.4%), shipped=1(14.3%), cancelled=1(14.3%).
solution_sql: SELECT status,COUNT(*)AS cnt,ROUND(COUNT(*)*100.0/(SELECT COUNT(*) FROM orders),1)AS pct FROM orders GROUP BY status ORDER BY cnt DESC;

title: Payment Resolution: Time to Retry
problem_statement:
For users who had a failed payment followed by a successful one, calculate the time gap in days between failure and next success.

Return user name, failed_date, next_success_date, days_gap. Order by days_gap.
hint: LEAD on payments partitioned by user_id, filter failed→success pairs.
explanation: Bhavna: failed Jan12, success Jan13 = 1 day. Chirag: failed Jan22, success Jan22 = 0 days.
solution_sql: WITH sequenced AS(SELECT p.*,LEAD(pay_date) OVER(PARTITION BY user_id ORDER BY pay_date,id)AS next_date,LEAD(status) OVER(PARTITION BY user_id ORDER BY pay_date,id)AS next_status FROM payments p)SELECT u.name,s.pay_date AS failed_date,s.next_date AS next_success_date,CAST(JULIANDAY(s.next_date)-JULIANDAY(s.pay_date)AS INT)AS days_gap FROM sequenced s JOIN pay_users u ON s.user_id=u.id WHERE s.status='failed' AND s.next_status='success' ORDER BY days_gap;

title: Payment Method Preference Per User
problem_statement:
Find each user's most-used payment method (by count of successful payments).

Return user name, preferred_method, and usage_count. Order by name.
hint: GROUP BY user_id, method → ROW_NUMBER per user → rn=1.
explanation: Aarav prefers UPI (2 times). Bhavna: UPI(1) vs card(1), pick first.
solution_sql: SELECT u.name,sub.method AS preferred_method,sub.usage_count FROM(SELECT user_id,method,COUNT(*)AS usage_count,ROW_NUMBER() OVER(PARTITION BY user_id ORDER BY COUNT(*) DESC)AS rn FROM payments WHERE status='success' GROUP BY user_id,method)sub JOIN pay_users u ON sub.user_id=u.id WHERE sub.rn=1 ORDER BY u.name;

title: Session Gap: Days Between Logins
problem_statement:
For each user, calculate the average gap (in days) between consecutive logins.

Return user name and avg_gap (rounded to 1). Order by avg_gap descending. Exclude users with only 1 login.
hint: LAG to get previous login, JULIANDAY diff, then AVG per user.
explanation: Diya: 1 login on Jan10, 1 on Jan20 = gap of 10 days avg. Aarav: gaps 1,1,3 = avg 1.7.
solution_sql: WITH gaps AS(SELECT user_id,JULIANDAY(login_date)-JULIANDAY(LAG(login_date) OVER(PARTITION BY user_id ORDER BY login_date))AS gap FROM(SELECT DISTINCT user_id,login_date FROM logins))SELECT u.name,ROUND(AVG(g.gap),1)AS avg_gap FROM gaps g JOIN users u ON g.user_id=u.id WHERE g.gap IS NOT NULL GROUP BY u.name ORDER BY avg_gap DESC;

title: Recursive CTE: Org Chart Levels
problem_statement:
Using a recursive CTE, simulate organizational levels. Assign level 1 to the highest-paid employee, level 2 to next tier, etc., based on salary bands (each 10K range).

Return name, salary, and org_level. Order by org_level, name.
hint: Recursive approach: tier = (max_salary - salary) / 10000 + 1.
explanation: Alice(70K)=L1. Eve(65K)=L1. Bob(60K)=L2. Charlie(55K)=L2. Diana(50K)=L3. Frank(45K)=L3.
solution_sql: SELECT name,salary,CAST((((SELECT MAX(salary) FROM employees)-salary)/10000)+1 AS INT)AS org_level FROM employees ORDER BY org_level,name;

title: Suspicious Transactions: Above 3x Personal Average
problem_statement:
Flag successful transactions where the amount exceeds 3 times the user's own average successful payment.

Return user name, pay_date, amount, user_avg (rounded), and multiplier (rounded to 1). Order by multiplier descending.
hint: JOIN payments to user avg subquery, filter amount > 3 * avg.
explanation: Diya: avg=(5000)/1=5000, so nothing exceeds 3x. Chirag: avg=1300, 2500 is 1.9x (below 3x).
solution_sql: SELECT u.name,p.pay_date,p.amount,ROUND(ua.avg_amt)AS user_avg,ROUND(p.amount/ua.avg_amt,1)AS multiplier FROM payments p JOIN pay_users u ON p.user_id=u.id JOIN(SELECT user_id,AVG(amount)AS avg_amt FROM payments WHERE status='success' GROUP BY user_id)ua ON p.user_id=ua.user_id WHERE p.status='success' AND p.amount>3*ua.avg_amt ORDER BY multiplier DESC;

title: YoY Transaction Volume Change
problem_statement:
Compare total transaction volume per account type (savings/current) between different months, simulating YoY.

Return acc_type, total_volume, avg_per_txn (rounded). Order by total_volume descending.
hint: GROUP BY type with SUM and AVG.
explanation: Savings vs Current account total volumes.
solution_sql: SELECT a.type AS acc_type,SUM(t.amount)AS total_volume,ROUND(AVG(t.amount),2)AS avg_per_txn FROM accounts a JOIN transactions t ON a.id=t.acc_id GROUP BY a.type ORDER BY total_volume DESC;

title: Employees Joined in Last 6 Months
problem_statement:
Find employees who joined after June 2023 (hire_date > '2023-06-01'). Since our schema uses a static dataset, find employees with salary below the median as a proxy for 'recent hires'.

Return name and salary. Order by salary.
hint: WHERE salary < (median). Median of 6 values = avg of 3rd and 4th.
explanation: Median = (60000+55000)/2 = 57500. Below: Frank(45K), Diana(50K), Charlie(55K).
solution_sql: SELECT name,salary FROM employees WHERE salary<(SELECT AVG(salary) FROM(SELECT salary FROM employees ORDER BY salary LIMIT 2 OFFSET 2))ORDER BY salary;

title: Department With Highest Average Salary
problem_statement:
Find the department with the highest average salary.

Return department and avg_salary (rounded to 0).
hint: GROUP BY department, ORDER BY AVG DESC, LIMIT 1.
explanation: Engineering: (70K+65K+55K)/3=63333. Marketing: (60K+50K)/2=55000. Sales: 45000.
solution_sql: SELECT department,ROUND(AVG(salary))AS avg_salary FROM employees GROUP BY department ORDER BY avg_salary DESC LIMIT 1;

title: Rider's 3rd Ride Ever
problem_statement:
Find each rider's 3rd ride (by date). Only include riders who have at least 3 rides.

Return rider name, ride_date, fare. Order by name.
hint: ROW_NUMBER() OVER (PARTITION BY rider_id ORDER BY ride_date) = 3.
explanation: Aarav has 4 rides. His 3rd: Feb 01, 200.
solution_sql: SELECT r2.name,r.ride_date,r.fare FROM(SELECT *,ROW_NUMBER() OVER(PARTITION BY rider_id ORDER BY ride_date)AS rn FROM rides)r JOIN riders r2 ON r.rider_id=r2.id WHERE r.rn=3 ORDER BY r2.name;

title: Users Inactive for 7+ Days
problem_statement:
Find users whose gap between any two consecutive logins exceeds 7 days.

Return distinct user name and max_gap. Order by max_gap descending.
hint: LAG + JULIANDAY diff, filter > 7.
explanation: Diya: Jan10→Jan20 = 10 day gap. Others have shorter gaps.
solution_sql: WITH gaps AS(SELECT user_id,CAST(JULIANDAY(login_date)-JULIANDAY(LAG(login_date) OVER(PARTITION BY user_id ORDER BY login_date))AS INT)AS gap FROM(SELECT DISTINCT user_id,login_date FROM logins))SELECT u.name,MAX(g.gap)AS max_gap FROM gaps g JOIN users u ON g.user_id=u.id WHERE g.gap>7 GROUP BY u.name ORDER BY max_gap DESC;

title: Pareto: Top 20% Revenue Customers
problem_statement:
Find customers who contribute to the top 20% of total revenue (Pareto principle).

Return name, total_spent, and cumulative_pct (rounded to 1). Show only those within top 20%.
hint: Cumulative SUM / grand total, filter <= 20.
explanation: With 5 customers, top 20% ≈ top 1 customer.
solution_sql: WITH customer_rev AS(SELECT c.name,SUM(o.total)AS total_spent FROM customers c JOIN orders o ON c.id=o.customer_id GROUP BY c.name),ranked AS(SELECT *,SUM(total_spent) OVER(ORDER BY total_spent DESC)AS cum_sum,(SELECT SUM(total) FROM orders)AS grand_total FROM customer_rev)SELECT name,total_spent,ROUND(cum_sum*100.0/grand_total,1)AS cumulative_pct FROM ranked WHERE ROUND(cum_sum*100.0/grand_total,1)<=50;

title: Extract Email Domain
problem_statement:
Extract just the domain from each customer's email address.

Return name and email_domain. Order by name.
hint: Use SUBSTR + INSTR to get everything after '@'.
explanation: ankit@gmail.com → gmail.com.
solution_sql: SELECT name,SUBSTR(email,INSTR(email,'@')+1)AS email_domain FROM customers ORDER BY name;

title: Cross-Month Retention
problem_statement:
Find users who logged in during BOTH the first week (Jan 10-16) AND the second week (Jan 17-23) of January 2024.

Return distinct user name.
hint: INTERSECT of two date-range queries, or HAVING COUNT(DISTINCT week)=2.
explanation: Who logged in both weeks?
