solution_sql: SELECT DISTINCT u.name FROM users u WHERE u.id IN(SELECT user_id FROM logins WHERE login_date BETWEEN '2024-01-10' AND '2024-01-16')AND u.id IN(SELECT user_id FROM logins WHERE login_date BETWEEN '2024-01-17' AND '2024-01-23')ORDER BY u.name;

title: Products With No Sales
problem_statement:
Find products that have never been ordered.

Return product_name.
hint: LEFT JOIN products to order_items WHERE order_item is NULL.
explanation: All products may have been ordered... let's check.
solution_sql: SELECT p.product_name FROM products p LEFT JOIN order_items oi ON p.id=oi.product_id WHERE oi.id IS NULL;

title: Multi-Condition CASE: Risk Rating
problem_statement:
Assign a risk rating to each account based on balance: 'High Risk' (balance < 30000), 'Medium Risk' (30000-60000), 'Low Risk' (>60000).

Return holder, balance, and risk_rating. Order by balance.
hint: CASE WHEN with multiple conditions.
explanation: Categorize each account.
solution_sql: SELECT holder,balance,CASE WHEN balance<30000 THEN 'High Risk' WHEN balance<=60000 THEN 'Medium Risk' ELSE 'Low Risk' END AS risk_rating FROM accounts ORDER BY balance;

title: Doctors With Most Appointments
problem_statement:
Find the doctor with the most appointments.

Return doctor name and appointment_count.
hint: JOIN doctors to appointments, GROUP BY, ORDER BY COUNT DESC, LIMIT 1.
explanation: Which doctor is busiest?
solution_sql: SELECT d.name,COUNT(*)AS appointment_count FROM doctors d JOIN appointments a ON d.id=a.doc_id GROUP BY d.name ORDER BY appointment_count DESC LIMIT 1;

title: Customer Lifetime Value Ranking
problem_statement:
Rank customers by their lifetime value (total spending minus cancelled orders). Include order count.

Return name, total_orders, lifetime_value, clv_rank. Order by clv_rank.
hint: Filter out cancelled, SUM total, DENSE_RANK by value.
explanation: Exclude cancelled orders from total.
solution_sql: WITH clv AS(SELECT c.name,COUNT(*)AS total_orders,SUM(o.total)AS lifetime_value FROM customers c JOIN orders o ON c.id=o.customer_id WHERE o.status!='cancelled' GROUP BY c.name)SELECT name,total_orders,lifetime_value,DENSE_RANK() OVER(ORDER BY lifetime_value DESC)AS clv_rank FROM clv ORDER BY clv_rank;

title: Viewers Who Watched All Genres
problem_statement:
Find users who have watched shows from every genre in the shows table.

Return user_name.
hint: COUNT(DISTINCT genre) per user = total distinct genres.
explanation: Check who has coverage across all genres.
solution_sql: SELECT u.user_name FROM users u JOIN watch_history w ON u.id=w.user_id JOIN shows s ON w.show_id=s.id GROUP BY u.user_name HAVING COUNT(DISTINCT s.genre)=(SELECT COUNT(DISTINCT genre) FROM shows);

title: Account Balance After All Transactions
problem_statement:
Calculate each account's effective balance after applying all credits and debits.

Return holder, original_balance, credits, debits, effective_balance. Order by effective_balance descending.
hint: Balance + SUM(credits) - SUM(debits).
explanation: Start with initial balance, add credits, subtract debits.
solution_sql: SELECT a.holder,a.balance AS original_balance,IFNULL(SUM(CASE WHEN t.type='credit' THEN t.amount END),0)AS credits,IFNULL(SUM(CASE WHEN t.type='debit' THEN t.amount END),0)AS debits,a.balance+IFNULL(SUM(CASE WHEN t.type='credit' THEN t.amount WHEN t.type='debit' THEN -t.amount END),0)AS effective_balance FROM accounts a LEFT JOIN transactions t ON a.id=t.acc_id GROUP BY a.id ORDER BY effective_balance DESC;

title: Weekday vs Weekend Orders
problem_statement:
Categorize orders into 'Weekday' vs 'Weekend' based on order_date and compare revenue.

Return day_type, order_count, total_revenue. Order by total_revenue descending.
hint: strftime('%w') gives day-of-week (0=Sun, 6=Sat). Weekend = 0 or 6.
explanation: Classify and aggregate.
solution_sql: SELECT CASE WHEN CAST(strftime('%w',order_date)AS INT) IN(0,6) THEN 'Weekend' ELSE 'Weekday' END AS day_type,COUNT(*)AS order_count,SUM(amount)AS total_revenue FROM orders GROUP BY day_type ORDER BY total_revenue DESC;

title: Signup-to-First-Login Time
problem_statement:
Calculate the number of days between each user's signup date and their first login.

Return name, signup_date, first_login, days_to_activate. Order by days_to_activate descending.
hint: MIN(login_date) per user, then JULIANDAY diff from signup.
explanation: How long until users become active?
solution_sql: SELECT u.name,u.signup_date,MIN(l.login_date)AS first_login,CAST(JULIANDAY(MIN(l.login_date))-JULIANDAY(u.signup_date)AS INT)AS days_to_activate FROM users u JOIN logins l ON u.id=l.user_id GROUP BY u.id ORDER BY days_to_activate DESC;

title: Top Spender Per City
problem_statement:
Find the highest spender (successful payments) in each city.

Return city, name, total_spent. Order by total_spent descending.
hint: CTE for user spending, ROW_NUMBER per city.
explanation: Mumbai: Aarav(3300) vs Eshan(350). Delhi: Bhavna. etc.
solution_sql: WITH user_spend AS(SELECT u.name,u.city,SUM(p.amount)AS total_spent FROM pay_users u JOIN payments p ON u.id=p.user_id WHERE p.status='success' GROUP BY u.id),ranked AS(SELECT *,ROW_NUMBER() OVER(PARTITION BY city ORDER BY total_spent DESC)AS rn FROM user_spend)SELECT city,name,total_spent FROM ranked WHERE rn=1 ORDER BY total_spent DESC;

title: Payment Volume MoM Growth
problem_statement:
Calculate the month-over-month growth in total successful payment volume.

Return month_num, volume, prev_volume, growth_pct (rounded to 1). Order by month.
hint: CTE for monthly SUM, LAG for previous month.
explanation: Jan total vs Feb total vs Mar total.
solution_sql: WITH monthly AS(SELECT strftime('%m',pay_date)AS month_num,SUM(amount)AS volume FROM payments WHERE status='success' GROUP BY strftime('%m',pay_date))SELECT month_num,volume,LAG(volume) OVER(ORDER BY month_num)AS prev_volume,ROUND((volume-LAG(volume) OVER(ORDER BY month_num))*100.0/LAG(volume) OVER(ORDER BY month_num),1)AS growth_pct FROM monthly ORDER BY month_num;

title: Average Rides Per Rider Per Month
problem_statement:
Calculate the average number of rides per active rider per month.

Return month, total_rides, active_riders, avg_rides_per_rider (rounded to 1). Order by month.
hint: COUNT rides / COUNT DISTINCT riders per month.
explanation: Jan: 4 rides by 3 riders = 1.3.
solution_sql: SELECT strftime('%m',ride_date)AS month,COUNT(*)AS total_rides,COUNT(DISTINCT rider_id)AS active_riders,ROUND(COUNT(*)*1.0/COUNT(DISTINCT rider_id),1)AS avg_rides_per_rider FROM rides GROUP BY strftime('%m',ride_date) ORDER BY month;

title: Multi-Schema Summary Dashboard
problem_statement:
Create a single-row executive dashboard showing: total employees, total departments, avg salary (rounded), highest salary, lowest salary, salary range, and total payroll.

Return all 7 metrics.
hint: Multiple aggregations in a single SELECT.
explanation: Comprehensive summary in one query.
solution_sql: SELECT COUNT(*)AS total_emp,COUNT(DISTINCT department)AS total_dept,ROUND(AVG(salary))AS avg_salary,MAX(salary)AS max_salary,MIN(salary)AS min_salary,MAX(salary)-MIN(salary)AS salary_range,SUM(salary)AS total_payroll FROM employees;

title: Grand Analytics: User 360° View
problem_statement:
Build a comprehensive user profile: name, city, total transactions, successful count, failed count, success rate, total spent, avg transaction, preferred method, and spending tier.

Return all metrics. Order by total_spent descending.
hint: Multi-CTE pipeline combining aggregation, conditional counts, ROW_NUMBER for preferred method, and CASE for tier.
explanation: Complete 360° user analytics.
solution_sql: WITH stats AS(SELECT u.name,u.city,COUNT(*)AS total_txns,SUM(CASE WHEN p.status='success' THEN 1 ELSE 0 END)AS success_cnt,SUM(CASE WHEN p.status='failed' THEN 1 ELSE 0 END)AS fail_cnt,ROUND(SUM(CASE WHEN p.status='success' THEN 1 ELSE 0 END)*100.0/COUNT(*),1)AS success_rate,IFNULL(SUM(CASE WHEN p.status='success' THEN p.amount END),0)AS total_spent,ROUND(IFNULL(AVG(CASE WHEN p.status='success' THEN p.amount END),0))AS avg_txn FROM pay_users u JOIN payments p ON u.id=p.user_id GROUP BY u.id),prefs AS(SELECT user_id,method,ROW_NUMBER() OVER(PARTITION BY user_id ORDER BY COUNT(*) DESC)AS rn FROM payments WHERE status='success' GROUP BY user_id,method)SELECT s.name,s.city,s.total_txns,s.success_cnt,s.fail_cnt,s.success_rate,s.total_spent,s.avg_txn,pr.method AS preferred_method,CASE WHEN s.total_spent>3000 THEN 'Platinum' WHEN s.total_spent>=1000 THEN 'Gold' ELSE 'Silver' END AS tier FROM stats s JOIN prefs pr ON s.name=(SELECT name FROM pay_users WHERE id=pr.user_id) AND pr.rn=1 ORDER BY s.total_spent DESC;

title: EXCEPT: Riders Not In January
problem_statement:
Find riders who took rides in February or March but NOT in January.

Return distinct rider name. Order by name.
hint: EXCEPT: Feb/Mar riders minus Jan riders.
explanation: Who started riding after January?
solution_sql: SELECT DISTINCT r2.name FROM rides r JOIN riders r2 ON r.rider_id=r2.id WHERE strftime('%m',r.ride_date) IN('02','03') EXCEPT SELECT DISTINCT r2.name FROM rides r JOIN riders r2 ON r.rider_id=r2.id WHERE strftime('%m',r.ride_date)='01' ORDER BY name;

title: EXISTS: Departments With High Earners
problem_statement:
Find departments that have at least one employee earning more than 60000 using EXISTS.

Return distinct department.
hint: WHERE EXISTS (SELECT 1 FROM employees e2 WHERE e2.department = e.department AND e2.salary > 60000).
explanation: Engineering has Alice(70K) and Eve(65K).
solution_sql: SELECT DISTINCT department FROM employees e WHERE EXISTS(SELECT 1 FROM employees e2 WHERE e2.department=e.department AND e2.salary>60000);

title: HAVING Multiple Conditions
problem_statement:
Find product categories where the total revenue exceeds 5000 AND the average order amount exceeds 1000.

Return category, total_revenue, avg_order (rounded). Order by total_revenue descending.
hint: GROUP BY category, HAVING SUM > 5000 AND AVG > 1000.
explanation: Electronics has high revenue and avg.
solution_sql: SELECT p.category,SUM(o.total)AS total_revenue,ROUND(AVG(o.total))AS avg_order FROM orders o JOIN order_items oi ON o.id=oi.order_id JOIN products p ON oi.product_id=p.id GROUP BY p.category HAVING SUM(o.total)>5000 AND AVG(o.total)>1000 ORDER BY total_revenue DESC;

title: Conditional ORDER BY
problem_statement:
Sort employees: Engineers first (by salary descending), then others (by name ascending).

Return name, department, salary.
hint: ORDER BY CASE to assign priority to Engineering, then salary/name.
explanation: Custom sorting logic.
solution_sql: SELECT name,department,salary FROM employees ORDER BY CASE WHEN department='Engineering' THEN 0 ELSE 1 END,CASE WHEN department='Engineering' THEN -salary ELSE 0 END,name;

title: Pivot: Monthly Revenue Columns
problem_statement:
Pivot monthly revenue into columns: show each customer with their Jan, Feb, Mar spending as separate columns.

Return name, jan_total, feb_total, mar_total. Order by name.
hint: Use SUM(CASE WHEN month='01' THEN total END) for each month.
explanation: Pivot long data to wide format.
solution_sql: SELECT c.name,SUM(CASE WHEN strftime('%m',o.order_date)='01' THEN o.total END)AS jan_total,SUM(CASE WHEN strftime('%m',o.order_date)='02' THEN o.total END)AS feb_total,SUM(CASE WHEN strftime('%m',o.order_date)='03' THEN o.total END)AS mar_total FROM customers c JOIN orders o ON c.id=o.customer_id GROUP BY c.name ORDER BY c.name;

title: Percentage Change: Salary vs Company Average
problem_statement:
For each employee, show how much their salary deviates from the company-wide average, as a percentage.

Return name, salary, company_avg (rounded), deviation_pct (rounded to 1). Order by deviation_pct descending.
hint: (salary - avg) / avg * 100.
solution_sql: SELECT name,salary,ROUND((SELECT AVG(salary) FROM employees))AS company_avg,ROUND((salary-(SELECT AVG(salary) FROM employees))*100.0/(SELECT AVG(salary) FROM employees),1)AS deviation_pct FROM employees ORDER BY deviation_pct DESC;

title: NULL-Safe Join: All Patients With Optional Appointments
problem_statement:
List ALL patients with their appointment details. Patients without appointments should still appear with NULL.

Return patient name, diagnosis, appointment_date. Order by name.
hint: LEFT JOIN patients to appointments.
explanation: Shows all patients, even those without appointments.
solution_sql: SELECT p.name,p.diagnosis,a.appointment_date FROM patients p LEFT JOIN appointments a ON p.id=a.patient_id ORDER BY p.name;

title: String Report: Comma-Separated Employees
problem_statement:
For each department, create a comma-separated list of employee names.

Return department and employee_list. Order by department.
hint: Use GROUP_CONCAT(name) grouped by department.
explanation: Engineering: Alice, Charlie, Eve.
solution_sql: SELECT department,GROUP_CONCAT(name)AS employee_list FROM(SELECT department,name FROM employees ORDER BY name)GROUP BY department ORDER BY department;

title: Median Salary Calculation
problem_statement:
Calculate the median salary from the employees table.

Return median_salary.
hint: For even N: average of middle two. N=6, median = avg of 3rd and 4th.
solution_sql: SELECT AVG(salary)AS median_salary FROM(SELECT salary,ROW_NUMBER() OVER(ORDER BY salary)AS rn,COUNT(*) OVER()AS cnt FROM employees)WHERE rn IN(cnt/2,cnt/2+1);

title: Complex Analytics: Rider Engagement Score
problem_statement:
Calculate a rider engagement score: (ride_count * 10) + (avg_fare * 0.1) + (distinct_drivers * 5). Rank riders by score.

Return name, ride_count, avg_fare (rounded), distinct_drivers, score (rounded), and engagement_rank.
hint: Multi-metric formula combined into a single score.
explanation: Weighted engagement scoring.
solution_sql: WITH metrics AS(SELECT r.rider_id,COUNT(*)AS ride_count,ROUND(AVG(r.fare))AS avg_fare,COUNT(DISTINCT r.driver_id)AS distinct_drivers FROM rides r WHERE r.status='completed' GROUP BY r.rider_id),scored AS(SELECT m.*,ROUND(m.ride_count*10+m.avg_fare*0.1+m.distinct_drivers*5)AS score FROM metrics m)SELECT r2.name,s.ride_count,s.avg_fare,s.distinct_drivers,s.score,DENSE_RANK() OVER(ORDER BY s.score DESC)AS engagement_rank FROM scored s JOIN riders r2 ON s.rider_id=r2.id ORDER BY engagement_rank;

title: BETWEEN With NOT: Exclude Mid-Range
problem_statement:
Find employees whose salary is NOT between 50000 and 65000.

Return name and salary. Order by salary.
hint: WHERE salary NOT BETWEEN 50000 AND 65000.
explanation: Frank(45K) and Alice(70K) are outside the range.
solution_sql: SELECT name,salary FROM employees WHERE salary NOT BETWEEN 50000 AND 65000 ORDER BY salary;

title: Count Distinct With Condition
problem_statement:
Count how many distinct users logged in more than twice in January 2024.

Return active_power_users.
hint: Subquery: GROUP BY user_id HAVING COUNT >= 3, outer COUNT.
explanation: Users with 3+ logins.
solution_sql: SELECT COUNT(*)AS active_power_users FROM(SELECT user_id FROM logins WHERE login_date BETWEEN '2024-01-01' AND '2024-01-31' GROUP BY user_id HAVING COUNT(*)>=3);

title: Year-Month Grouping
problem_statement:
Group transactions by year-month and show count and total per month.

Return year_month, txn_count, total_amount. Order by year_month.
hint: strftime('%Y-%m', date) for year-month grouping.
explanation: Monthly transaction summary.
solution_sql: SELECT strftime('%Y-%m',txn_date)AS year_month,COUNT(*)AS txn_count,SUM(amount)AS total_amount FROM transactions GROUP BY strftime('%Y-%m',txn_date) ORDER BY year_month;

title: Customers Who Ordered Every Month
problem_statement:
Find customers who placed at least one order in every month available in the data (Jan, Feb, Mar).

Return customer name.
hint: COUNT(DISTINCT month) = total distinct months.
explanation: Who ordered in all 3 months?
solution_sql: SELECT c.name FROM customers c JOIN orders o ON c.id=o.customer_id GROUP BY c.name HAVING COUNT(DISTINCT strftime('%m',o.order_date))=(SELECT COUNT(DISTINCT strftime('%m',order_date)) FROM orders);

title: Cumulative User Signups
problem_statement:
Show a cumulative count of user signups over time.

Return signup_date and cumulative_users. Order by signup_date.
hint: COUNT(*) OVER (ORDER BY signup_date).
explanation: Running total of new signups.
solution_sql: SELECT signup_date,COUNT(*) OVER(ORDER BY signup_date)AS cumulative_users FROM users ORDER BY signup_date;

title: Window: Difference From Previous Row
problem_statement:
For Aarav's successful payments, show each payment and the difference from the previous payment amount.

Return pay_date, amount, prev_amount, difference. Order by pay_date.
hint: LAG(amount) OVER(ORDER BY pay_date), then subtract.
solution_sql: SELECT pay_date,amount,LAG(amount) OVER(ORDER BY pay_date)AS prev_amount,amount-LAG(amount) OVER(ORDER BY pay_date)AS difference FROM payments WHERE user_id=1 AND status='success' ORDER BY pay_date;

title: INTERSECT: Users Who Logged In Jan 10 AND Jan 11
problem_statement:
Find users who logged in on BOTH January 10 AND January 11 using INTERSECT.

Return name. Order by name.
hint: SELECT user_id FROM logins WHERE date='Jan10' INTERSECT SELECT user_id FROM logins WHERE date='Jan11'.
explanation: Users active on both consecutive days.
solution_sql: SELECT u.name FROM users u WHERE u.id IN(SELECT user_id FROM logins WHERE login_date='2024-01-10' INTERSECT SELECT user_id FROM logins WHERE login_date='2024-01-11')ORDER BY u.name;

title: Conditional Aggregation: Pass/Fail Count
problem_statement:
Count students who passed (grade A or B) vs failed (grade C or below) per course.

Return course_name, passed, failed. Order by course_name.
hint: SUM(CASE WHEN grade IN('A','B') THEN 1 ELSE 0 END).
explanation: Pass/fail breakdown per course.
solution_sql: SELECT c.course_name,SUM(CASE WHEN e.grade IN('A','B') THEN 1 ELSE 0 END)AS passed,SUM(CASE WHEN e.grade NOT IN('A','B') THEN 1 ELSE 0 END)AS failed FROM courses c JOIN enrollments e ON c.id=e.course_id GROUP BY c.course_name ORDER BY c.course_name;

title: Dense Data: Fill Gaps in Date Sequence
problem_statement:
Identify which dates in January 10-20 had NO logins at all.

Return missing_date.
hint: Generate date sequence, LEFT JOIN to logins, filter NULLs.
explanation: Which days had zero activity?
solution_sql: WITH RECURSIVE dates(d) AS(SELECT '2024-01-10' UNION ALL SELECT DATE(d,'+1 day') FROM dates WHERE d<'2024-01-20')SELECT d AS missing_date FROM dates WHERE d NOT IN(SELECT DISTINCT login_date FROM logins) ORDER BY d;

title: Grand Finale: Cross-Schema Business Intelligence
problem_statement:
Build a comprehensive BI report from the E-Commerce schema: for each customer, show their name, city, email domain, total orders, total spent, avg order (rounded), first order date, last order date, days as customer, favorite category, and customer segment (VIP/Regular/New).

Return all metrics. Order by total_spent descending.
hint: Multi-CTE pipeline: customer stats + favorite category + segmentation.
explanation: The ultimate analytical query combining everything learned.
solution_sql: WITH stats AS(SELECT c.id,c.name,c.city,SUBSTR(c.email,INSTR(c.email,'@')+1)AS domain,COUNT(*)AS orders,SUM(o.total)AS spent,ROUND(AVG(o.total))AS avg_order,MIN(o.order_date)AS first_order,MAX(o.order_date)AS last_order,CAST(JULIANDAY(MAX(o.order_date))-JULIANDAY(MIN(o.order_date))AS INT)AS days_active FROM customers c JOIN orders o ON c.id=o.customer_id WHERE o.status!='cancelled' GROUP BY c.id),cats AS(SELECT o.customer_id,p.category,SUM(oi.qty)AS qty,ROW_NUMBER() OVER(PARTITION BY o.customer_id ORDER BY SUM(oi.qty) DESC)AS rn FROM orders o JOIN order_items oi ON o.id=oi.order_id JOIN products p ON oi.product_id=p.id WHERE o.status!='cancelled' GROUP BY o.customer_id,p.category)SELECT s.name,s.city,s.domain,s.orders,s.spent,s.avg_order,s.first_order,s.last_order,s.days_active,cat.category AS top_category,CASE WHEN s.spent>10000 THEN 'VIP' WHEN s.spent>1000 THEN 'Regular' ELSE 'New' END AS segment FROM stats s LEFT JOIN cats cat ON s.id=cat.customer_id AND cat.rn=1 ORDER BY s.spent DESC;

title: Employee Earns More Than Manager
problem_statement:
Find employees who earn MORE than their direct manager.

Return employee name, employee salary, manager name, manager salary. Order by employee salary DESC.
hint: Self-join: employees e1 JOIN employees e2 ON e1.mgr_id = e2.id WHERE e1.salary > e2.salary.
explanation: Bob(60K) has manager Alice(70K) — no. Eve(65K) has manager Alice(70K) — no. Diana(50K) has manager Charlie(55K) — no.
solution_sql: SELECT e1.name AS emp_name,e1.salary AS emp_salary,e2.name AS mgr_name,e2.salary AS mgr_salary FROM employees e1 JOIN employees e2 ON e1.mgr_id=e2.id WHERE e1.salary>e2.salary ORDER BY e1.salary DESC;

title: Odd-Numbered Rows Only
problem_statement:
Select only odd-numbered rows from the employees table (by id).

Return id, name. Order by id.
hint: Use MOD or % operator: WHERE id % 2 != 0.
explanation: IDs 1,3,5 are odd.
solution_sql: SELECT id,name FROM employees WHERE id%2!=0 ORDER BY id;

title: Departments With More Than 2 Employees
problem_statement:
Find departments that have more than 2 employees.

Return dept, emp_count. Order by emp_count DESC.
hint: GROUP BY dept HAVING COUNT(*) > 2.
explanation: Eng has 3 employees (Alice, Bob, Eve).
solution_sql: SELECT dept,COUNT(*)AS emp_count FROM employees GROUP BY dept HAVING COUNT(*)>2 ORDER BY emp_count DESC;

title: Employees Hired Before 2021
problem_statement:
Find all employees hired before January 1, 2021.

Return name, hire_date. Order by hire_date.
hint: WHERE hire_date < '2021-01-01'.
explanation: Alice(2019), Bob(2020), Charlie(2020).
solution_sql: SELECT name,hire_date FROM employees WHERE hire_date<'2021-01-01' ORDER BY hire_date;

title: Total Salary Expense Per Department
problem_statement:
Calculate the total salary expense for each department.

Return dept, total_salary. Order by total_salary DESC.
hint: GROUP BY dept + SUM(salary).
solution_sql: SELECT dept,SUM(salary)AS total_salary FROM employees GROUP BY dept ORDER BY total_salary DESC;

title: Salary Above Company Average
problem_statement:
Find employees whose salary is above the company-wide average.

Return name, salary. Order by salary DESC.
hint: Subquery: WHERE salary > (SELECT AVG(salary) FROM employees). Avg = 57500.
explanation: Alice(70K), Eve(65K), Bob(60K) are above 57500.
solution_sql: SELECT name,salary FROM employees WHERE salary>(SELECT AVG(salary) FROM employees) ORDER BY salary DESC;

title: Highest Salary Per Department
problem_statement:
Find the employee with the highest salary in each department.

Return dept, name, salary. Order by salary DESC.
hint: Use RANK() or DENSE_RANK() PARTITION BY dept ORDER BY salary DESC, filter rank=1.
explanation: Eng: Alice(70K), Sales: Charlie(55K), HR: Frank(45K).
solution_sql: SELECT dept,name,salary FROM(SELECT dept,name,salary,RANK() OVER(PARTITION BY dept ORDER BY salary DESC)AS rk FROM employees)t WHERE rk=1 ORDER BY salary DESC;

title: Employees Never Assigned a Project
problem_statement:
Find customers who have never placed an order.

Return name, city. Order by name.
hint: LEFT JOIN orders ON customers.id = orders.customer_id WHERE orders.id IS NULL.
explanation: All customers have at least one order in our dataset.
solution_sql: SELECT c.name,c.city FROM customers c LEFT JOIN orders o ON c.id=o.customer_id WHERE o.id IS NULL ORDER BY c.name;

title: DELETE vs TRUNCATE Simulation: Count After Filter
problem_statement:
Count how many employees would remain if we deleted all employees in 'HR' department.

Return remaining_count.
hint: COUNT with WHERE dept != 'HR'.
explanation: 6 total - 1 HR = 5.
solution_sql: SELECT COUNT(*)AS remaining_count FROM employees WHERE dept!='HR';

title: DISTINCT Departments
problem_statement:
List all unique departments from the employees table.

Return dept. Order alphabetically.
hint: SELECT DISTINCT dept.
explanation: Eng, HR, Sales.
solution_sql: SELECT DISTINCT dept FROM employees ORDER BY dept;

title: Employees With NULL Manager
problem_statement:
Find employees who have no manager (mgr_id is NULL).

Return name, dept. Order by name.
hint: WHERE mgr_id IS NULL.
explanation: Alice, Charlie, Frank have no manager.
solution_sql: SELECT name,dept FROM employees WHERE mgr_id IS NULL ORDER BY name;

title: COUNT(*) vs COUNT(column) Difference
problem_statement:
Show the difference between COUNT(*) and COUNT(mgr_id) on the employees table.

Return total_rows, non_null_managers.
hint: COUNT(*) counts all rows. COUNT(mgr_id) skips NULLs.
explanation: 6 total rows, 3 have non-null mgr_id.
solution_sql: SELECT COUNT(*)AS total_rows,COUNT(mgr_id)AS non_null_managers FROM employees;

title: Employees Joined in Each Year
problem_statement:
Count how many employees were hired in each year.

Return hire_year, count. Order by hire_year.
hint: Use SUBSTR or strftime to extract year from hire_date.
solution_sql: SELECT SUBSTR(hire_date,1,4)AS hire_year,COUNT(*)AS count FROM employees GROUP BY SUBSTR(hire_date,1,4) ORDER BY hire_year;

title: Min and Max Salary Per Department
problem_statement:
Find the minimum and maximum salary in each department.

Return dept, min_sal, max_sal. Order by dept.
hint: GROUP BY dept + MIN(salary) + MAX(salary).
explanation: Eng: 60K-70K. HR: 45K-45K. Sales: 50K-55K.
solution_sql: SELECT dept,MIN(salary)AS min_sal,MAX(salary)AS max_sal FROM employees GROUP BY dept ORDER BY dept;

title: Concatenate First and Last Name
problem_statement:
Create a full display name by combining username with ' (@' and username and ')' format.

Return profile display as 'name (@username)'. Order by username.
hint: Use || operator for string concatenation.
explanation: Builds display strings from profiles table.
solution_sql: SELECT username||' (@'||username||')'AS display FROM profiles ORDER BY username;

title: Average Salary Comparison: Above vs Below
problem_statement:
Classify each employee as 'Above Average' or 'Below Average' based on company-wide average salary.

Return name, salary, classification. Order by salary DESC.
hint: CASE WHEN salary > (SELECT AVG(salary)...) THEN 'Above Average' ELSE 'Below Average'.
explanation: Avg is 57500. Alice,Eve,Bob above. Charlie,Diana,Frank below.
solution_sql: SELECT name,salary,CASE WHEN salary>(SELECT AVG(salary) FROM employees) THEN 'Above Average' ELSE 'Below Average' END AS classification FROM employees ORDER BY salary DESC;

title: Employees Sharing Same Department as 'Alice'
problem_statement:
Find all employees in the same department as Alice (excluding Alice herself).

Return name, dept. Order by name.
hint: Subquery to get Alice's dept, then WHERE dept = that AND name != 'Alice'.
explanation: Bob and Eve are in Eng with Alice.
solution_sql: SELECT name,dept FROM employees WHERE dept=(SELECT dept FROM employees WHERE name='Alice') AND name!='Alice' ORDER BY name;

title: Multi-Table: Customer Orders With Product Names
problem_statement:
Join customers, orders, order_items, and products to show who bought what.

Return customer name, product name, qty, order_date. Order by order_date, customer name.
hint: 4-table join: customers -> orders -> order_items -> products.
explanation: Multi-table join across the full e-commerce schema.
solution_sql: SELECT c.name,p.name AS product_name,oi.qty,o.order_date FROM customers c JOIN orders o ON c.id=o.customer_id JOIN order_items oi ON o.id=oi.order_id JOIN products p ON oi.product_id=p.id ORDER BY o.order_date,c.name;

title: Weekend Orders Only
problem_statement:
Find orders placed on weekends (Saturday=6 or Sunday=0 in SQLite strftime('%w')).

Return order_id, order_date, day_of_week. Order by order_date.
hint: strftime('%w', order_date) returns 0 (Sun) to 6 (Sat).
explanation: Check which order dates fall on weekends.
solution_sql: SELECT id AS order_id,order_date,CASE CAST(strftime('%w',order_date)AS INT) WHEN 0 THEN 'Sunday' WHEN 6 THEN 'Saturday' END AS day_of_week FROM orders WHERE CAST(strftime('%w',order_date)AS INT) IN(0,6) ORDER BY order_date;

title: Customers With Orders Above Average Total
problem_statement:
Find customers whose order total exceeds the average order total across all orders.

Return customer name, order total. Order by total DESC.
hint: Subquery: WHERE o.total > (SELECT AVG(total) FROM orders).
explanation: Avg order total, then filter above it.
solution_sql: SELECT c.name,o.total FROM customers c JOIN orders o ON c.id=o.customer_id WHERE o.total>(SELECT AVG(total) FROM orders) ORDER BY o.total DESC;

title: UNION: Combine Riders and Drivers
problem_statement:
Create a unified list of all people in the ride-sharing system (both riders and drivers).

Return name, role ('Rider' or 'Driver'). Order by name.
hint: SELECT name, 'Rider' FROM riders UNION ALL SELECT name, 'Driver' FROM drivers.
explanation: Combines both tables with a role label.
solution_sql: SELECT name,'Rider'AS role FROM riders UNION ALL SELECT name,'Driver'AS role FROM drivers ORDER BY name;

title: Employees in Same City as Company HQ
problem_statement:
Find riders whose city matches any driver's city.

Return rider name, city. Order by name.
hint: WHERE city IN (SELECT city FROM drivers).
explanation: Check city overlap between riders and drivers.
solution_sql: SELECT r.name,r.city FROM riders r WHERE r.city IN(SELECT d.city FROM drivers d) ORDER BY r.name;

title: Revenue Trend: Orders Per Month
problem_statement:
Calculate total revenue and order count per month.

Return month, order_count, total_revenue. Order by month.
hint: GROUP BY SUBSTR(order_date,1,7) for year-month grouping.
explanation: Jan: 2 orders, Feb: 1, Mar: 4.
solution_sql: SELECT SUBSTR(order_date,1,7)AS month,COUNT(*)AS order_count,SUM(total)AS total_revenue FROM orders WHERE status!='cancelled' GROUP BY SUBSTR(order_date,1,7) ORDER BY month;

title: Customer Segmentation by Order Count
problem_statement:
Segment customers into 'Frequent' (3+ orders), 'Regular' (2 orders), or 'New' (1 order).

Return name, order_count, segment. Order by order_count DESC.
hint: CASE WHEN COUNT >= 3 THEN 'Frequent' WHEN COUNT = 2 THEN 'Regular' ELSE 'New'.
explanation: Ankit has 3 orders, others have 1.
solution_sql: SELECT c.name,COUNT(o.id)AS order_count,CASE WHEN COUNT(o.id)>=3 THEN 'Frequent' WHEN COUNT(o.id)=2 THEN 'Regular' ELSE 'New' END AS segment FROM customers c JOIN orders o ON c.id=o.customer_id WHERE o.status!='cancelled' GROUP BY c.id,c.name ORDER BY order_count DESC;

title: Library Schema: Books Not Borrowed
problem_statement:
Find courses that have zero enrollments.

Return course_name. Order by course_name.
hint: LEFT JOIN enrollments, WHERE enrollment.id IS NULL.
explanation: Check for courses without any student enrollment.
solution_sql: SELECT c.name AS course_name FROM courses c LEFT JOIN enrollments e ON c.id=e.course_id WHERE e.id IS NULL ORDER BY c.name;

title: Salary Raise Simulation: 10% Hike for Eng
problem_statement:
Show what each Engineering employee's salary would be after a 10% raise.

Return name, current_salary, new_salary. Order by new_salary DESC.
hint: SELECT name, salary, salary * 1.10 WHERE dept = 'Eng'.
solution_sql: SELECT name,salary AS current_salary,ROUND(salary*1.10,0)AS new_salary FROM employees WHERE dept='Eng' ORDER BY new_salary DESC;

title: Employees Hired Same Year as Alice
problem_statement:
Find employees hired in the same year as Alice.

Return name, hire_date. Order by name.
hint: Subquery: WHERE SUBSTR(hire_date,1,4) = (SELECT SUBSTR(hire_date,1,4) FROM employees WHERE name='Alice').
explanation: Alice hired 2019. Only Alice herself in 2019.
solution_sql: SELECT name,hire_date FROM employees WHERE SUBSTR(hire_date,1,4)=(SELECT SUBSTR(hire_date,1,4) FROM employees WHERE name='Alice') ORDER BY name;

title: Top 3 Highest Paid Employees
problem_statement:
Find the 3 highest-paid employees.

Return name, salary. Order by salary DESC.
hint: ORDER BY salary DESC LIMIT 3.
explanation: Alice(70K), Eve(65K), Bob(60K).
solution_sql: SELECT name,salary FROM employees ORDER BY salary DESC LIMIT 3;

title: Average Fare Per City
problem_statement:
Calculate the average ride fare for each city.

Return city, avg_fare (rounded to 1). Order by avg_fare DESC.
hint: JOIN rides with riders, GROUP BY city, AVG(fare).
explanation: Avg fare per city from the rides table.
solution_sql: SELECT r2.city,ROUND(AVG(r.fare),1)AS avg_fare FROM rides r JOIN riders r2 ON r.rider_id=r2.id GROUP BY r2.city ORDER BY avg_fare DESC;

title: Count Orders Per Customer
problem_statement:
Count how many orders each customer has placed.

Return name, order_count. Order by order_count DESC.
hint: JOIN customers + orders, GROUP BY, COUNT.
explanation: Ankit has 3, others have 1.
solution_sql: SELECT c.name,COUNT(o.id)AS order_count FROM customers c JOIN orders o ON c.id=o.customer_id GROUP BY c.name ORDER BY order_count DESC;

title: Products Never Ordered
problem_statement:
Find products that have never been ordered by anyone.

Return product name, price. Order by name.
hint: LEFT JOIN order_items ON product_id, WHERE order_items.id IS NULL.
explanation: Check which products have no entries in order_items.
solution_sql: SELECT p.name,p.price FROM products p LEFT JOIN order_items oi ON p.id=oi.product_id WHERE oi.id IS NULL ORDER BY p.name;

title: Display With Column Aliases
problem_statement:
Display employee info with friendly column names: 'Full Name', 'Department', 'Annual Salary'.

Return Full_Name, Department, Annual_Salary. Order by Annual_Salary DESC.
hint: Use AS for aliasing. salary is already annual.
explanation: Simple column aliasing exercise.
solution_sql: SELECT name AS Full_Name,dept AS Department,salary AS Annual_Salary FROM employees ORDER BY Annual_Salary DESC;

title: Employees Between Salary Range
problem_statement:
Find employees with salary between 50000 and 65000 (inclusive).

Return name, salary. Order by salary.
hint: WHERE salary BETWEEN 50000 AND 65000.
explanation: Charlie(55K), Diana(50K), Bob(60K), Eve(65K).
solution_sql: SELECT name,salary FROM employees WHERE salary BETWEEN 50000 AND 65000 ORDER BY salary;

title: Department With Lowest Average Salary
problem_statement:
Find the department with the lowest average salary.

Return dept, avg_salary.
hint: GROUP BY dept, ORDER BY AVG(salary) ASC LIMIT 1.
explanation: HR avg=45K, Sales avg=52.5K, Eng avg=65K. HR is lowest.
solution_sql: SELECT dept,AVG(salary)AS avg_salary FROM employees GROUP BY dept ORDER BY avg_salary ASC LIMIT 1;

title: Employees With 'a' in Name
problem_statement:
Find all employees whose name contains the letter 'a' (case-insensitive).

Return name. Order by name.
hint: WHERE LOWER(name) LIKE '%a%'.
explanation: Alice, Charlie, Diana, Frank, Eve — check lowercase.
solution_sql: SELECT name FROM employees WHERE LOWER(name) LIKE '%a%' ORDER BY name;

title: Total Revenue Per Restaurant
problem_statement:
Find total order revenue for each restaurant.

Return restaurant name, total_revenue. Order by total_revenue DESC.
hint: JOIN restaurants + orders, SUM(amount), GROUP BY.
explanation: Biryani House: 450+380+400=1230. Pizza Palace: 650+700=1350.
solution_sql: SELECT r.name,SUM(o.amount)AS total_revenue FROM restaurants r JOIN orders o ON r.id=o.rest_id GROUP BY r.name ORDER BY total_revenue DESC;

title: Customers From Mumbai or Delhi
problem_statement:
Find customers who live in Mumbai or Delhi.

Return name, city. Order by name.
hint: WHERE city IN ('Mumbai','Delhi').
explanation: Ankit=Mumbai, Priya=Delhi, Sneha=Mumbai.
solution_sql: SELECT name,city FROM customers WHERE city IN('Mumbai','Delhi') ORDER BY name;

title: Latest Hire Per Department
problem_statement:
Find the most recently hired employee in each department.

Return dept, name, hire_date. Order by dept.
hint: ROW_NUMBER() PARTITION BY dept ORDER BY hire_date DESC, filter rn=1.
explanation: Eng: Eve(2021-08). Sales: Diana(2021-01). HR: Frank(2022-02).
solution_sql: SELECT dept,name,hire_date FROM(SELECT dept,name,hire_date,ROW_NUMBER() OVER(PARTITION BY dept ORDER BY hire_date DESC)AS rn FROM employees)t WHERE rn=1 ORDER BY dept;

title: Count NULL vs Non-NULL Managers
problem_statement:
Count how many employees have a manager vs those who don't.

Return has_manager, count. Order by has_manager.
hint: CASE WHEN mgr_id IS NULL THEN 'No' ELSE 'Yes' + GROUP BY.
explanation: 3 with manager, 3 without.
solution_sql: SELECT CASE WHEN mgr_id IS NULL THEN 'No' ELSE 'Yes' END AS has_manager,COUNT(*)AS count FROM employees GROUP BY has_manager ORDER BY has_manager;

title: Rides With Above-Average Fare
problem_statement:
Find all rides where the fare exceeds the overall average fare.

Return rider name, fare. Order by fare DESC.
hint: Subquery: WHERE fare > (SELECT AVG(fare) FROM rides).
explanation: Filter rides above the average.
solution_sql: SELECT ri.name,r.fare FROM rides r JOIN riders ri ON r.rider_id=ri.id WHERE r.fare>(SELECT AVG(fare) FROM rides) ORDER BY r.fare DESC;

title: Orders in January 2024
problem_statement:
Find all orders placed in January 2024.

Return order_id, order_date, total. Order by order_date.
hint: WHERE order_date BETWEEN '2024-01-01' AND '2024-01-31'.
explanation: Orders 1,2,3 are in January.
solution_sql: SELECT id AS order_id,order_date,total FROM orders WHERE order_date BETWEEN '2024-01-01' AND '2024-01-31' ORDER BY order_date;

title: Rank Employees by Salary
problem_statement:
Assign a rank to each employee based on salary (highest=1). Use DENSE_RANK.

Return name, salary, salary_rank. Order by salary_rank.
hint: DENSE_RANK() OVER(ORDER BY salary DESC).
explanation: Alice=1, Eve=2, Bob=3, Charlie=4, Diana=5, Frank=6.
solution_sql: SELECT name,salary,DENSE_RANK() OVER(ORDER BY salary DESC)AS salary_rank FROM employees ORDER BY salary_rank;

title: Top 2 Cuisines by Restaurant Count
problem_statement:
Find the top 2 cuisines that have the most restaurants.

Return cuisine, restaurant_count. Order by count DESC.
hint: GROUP BY cuisine, COUNT, ORDER BY DESC LIMIT 2.
explanation: Indian=2, others=1 each.
solution_sql: SELECT cuisine,COUNT(*)AS restaurant_count FROM restaurants GROUP BY cuisine ORDER BY restaurant_count DESC LIMIT 2;

title: Patients With Exactly One Visit
problem_statement:
Find patients who visited the doctor exactly once.

Return name, visit_count. Order by name.
hint: GROUP BY patient_id HAVING COUNT = 1.
explanation: Priya and Sneha have exactly 1 appointment each.
solution_sql: SELECT p.name,COUNT(a.id)AS visit_count FROM patients p JOIN appointments a ON p.id=a.patient_id GROUP BY p.name HAVING COUNT(a.id)=1 ORDER BY p.name;

title: Total Employee Count
problem_statement:
Count the total number of employees in the company.

Return total_employees.
hint: Simple COUNT(*).
explanation: 6 employees total.
solution_sql: SELECT COUNT(*)AS total_employees FROM employees;

title: Average Order Value Per Customer
problem_statement:
Calculate the average order value for each customer.

Return name, avg_order_value (rounded to 0). Order by avg_order_value DESC.
hint: JOIN customers + orders, AVG(total), GROUP BY.
explanation: Rohan=55450, Ankit avg of 3 orders, etc.
solution_sql: SELECT c.name,ROUND(AVG(o.total),0)AS avg_order_value FROM customers c JOIN orders o ON c.id=o.customer_id GROUP BY c.name ORDER BY avg_order_value DESC;

title: Restaurants Rated Above 4.0
problem_statement:
Find all restaurants with a rating above 4.0.

Return name, rating. Order by rating DESC.
hint: WHERE rating > 4.0.
explanation: Dosa Corner(4.7), Biryani House(4.5), Pizza Palace(4.2).
solution_sql: SELECT name,rating FROM restaurants WHERE rating>4.0 ORDER BY rating DESC;

title: Count Rides Per Driver
problem_statement:
Count how many rides each driver has completed.

Return driver name, ride_count. Order by ride_count DESC.
hint: JOIN drivers + rides, GROUP BY, COUNT.
explanation: Count from rides table grouped by driver.
solution_sql: SELECT d.name,COUNT(r.id)AS ride_count FROM drivers d JOIN rides r ON d.id=r.driver_id GROUP BY d.name ORDER BY ride_count DESC;

title: Employees With Same Salary
problem_statement:
Find pairs of employees who have the exact same salary.

Return emp1, emp2, salary. Order by salary DESC.
hint: Self-join: e1.salary = e2.salary AND e1.id < e2.id.
explanation: Check for duplicate salary values.
solution_sql: SELECT e1.name AS emp1,e2.name AS emp2,e1.salary FROM employees e1 JOIN employees e2 ON e1.salary=e2.salary AND e1.id<e2.id ORDER BY e1.salary DESC;

title: Most Expensive Product Per Category
problem_statement:
Find the most expensive product in each category.

Return category, product name, price. Order by price DESC.
hint: ROW_NUMBER PARTITION BY category ORDER BY price DESC, rn=1.
explanation: Electronics: MacBook(120K). Fashion: Running Shoes(3499). Books: SQL Book(450).
solution_sql: SELECT category,name,price FROM(SELECT category,name,price,ROW_NUMBER() OVER(PARTITION BY category ORDER BY price DESC)AS rn FROM products)t WHERE rn=1 ORDER BY price DESC;

title: Customers Who Ordered Electronics
problem_statement:
Find customers who ordered at least one product in the 'Electronics' category.

Return DISTINCT customer name. Order by name.
hint: Multi-join: customers->orders->order_items->products WHERE category='Electronics'.
explanation: Join through 4 tables and filter by category.
solution_sql: SELECT DISTINCT c.name FROM customers c JOIN orders o ON c.id=o.customer_id JOIN order_items oi ON o.id=oi.order_id JOIN products p ON oi.product_id=p.id WHERE p.category='Electronics' ORDER BY c.name;

title: All Departments Even Empty Ones
problem_statement:
List all departments. If a department has employees, show the count; otherwise show 0.

Return dept, emp_count. Order by dept.
hint: Use a subquery or GROUP BY on existing data.
explanation: Eng=3, HR=1, Sales=2. All depts have employees here.
solution_sql: SELECT dept,COUNT(*)AS emp_count FROM employees GROUP BY dept ORDER BY dept;

title: Cancelled Orders
problem_statement:
Find all orders with status 'cancelled'.

Return order_id, customer_id, total. Order by order_id.
hint: WHERE status = 'cancelled'.
explanation: Check which orders are cancelled.
solution_sql: SELECT id AS order_id,customer_id,total FROM orders WHERE status='cancelled' ORDER BY id;

title: Driver With Highest Total Earnings
problem_statement:
Find the driver who earned the most total fare across all rides.

Return driver name, total_fare.
hint: JOIN drivers + rides, SUM(fare), ORDER BY DESC LIMIT 1.
explanation: Sum fares per driver and pick the top one.
solution_sql: SELECT d.name,SUM(r.fare)AS total_fare FROM drivers d JOIN rides r ON d.id=r.driver_id GROUP BY d.name ORDER BY total_fare DESC LIMIT 1;

title: Mark Old Orders as Archived
problem_statement:
Simulate marking orders before Feb 2024 as 'Archived' and others as 'Active'.

Return order_id, order_date, archive_status. Order by order_date.
hint: CASE WHEN order_date < '2024-02-01' THEN 'Archived' ELSE 'Active'.
explanation: Jan orders = Archived, Feb+ = Active.
solution_sql: SELECT id AS order_id,order_date,CASE WHEN order_date<'2024-02-01' THEN 'Archived' ELSE 'Active' END AS archive_status FROM orders ORDER BY order_date;

title: Average Rating Per Cuisine
problem_statement:
Calculate the average restaurant rating for each cuisine type.

Return cuisine, avg_rating (rounded to 1). Order by avg_rating DESC.
hint: GROUP BY cuisine, AVG(rating).
explanation: Indian avg=(4.5+4.7)/2=4.6. Italian=4.2. Chinese=3.8. American=3.5.
solution_sql: SELECT cuisine,ROUND(AVG(rating),1)AS avg_rating FROM restaurants GROUP BY cuisine ORDER BY avg_rating DESC;

title: Patients Diagnosed With Hypertension
problem_statement:
Find all patients who were diagnosed with Hypertension.

Return patient name, visit_date. Order by visit_date.
hint: JOIN patients + appointments WHERE diagnosis='Hypertension'.
explanation: Rahul diagnosed with Hypertension on 2024-01-10.
solution_sql: SELECT p.name,a.visit_date FROM patients p JOIN appointments a ON p.id=a.patient_id WHERE a.diagnosis='Hypertension' ORDER BY a.visit_date;

title: Rides in February 2024
problem_statement:
Find all rides that took place in February 2024.

Return ride_id, ride_date, fare. Order by ride_date.
hint: WHERE ride_date BETWEEN '2024-02-01' AND '2024-02-28'.
explanation: Filter rides by February date range.
solution_sql: SELECT id AS ride_id,ride_date,fare FROM rides WHERE ride_date BETWEEN '2024-02-01' AND '2024-02-29' ORDER BY ride_date;

title: Products Costing Above Average
problem_statement:
Find products whose price is above the average product price.

Return name, price. Order by price DESC.
hint: WHERE price > (SELECT AVG(price) FROM products).
explanation: Average price, then filter above it.
solution_sql: SELECT name,price FROM products WHERE price>(SELECT AVG(price) FROM products) ORDER BY price DESC;

title: Student With Highest GPA
problem_statement:
Find the student with the highest GPA.

Return student name, gpa.
hint: ORDER BY gpa DESC LIMIT 1.
explanation: Find top GPA student.
solution_sql: SELECT s.name,s.gpa FROM students s ORDER BY s.gpa DESC LIMIT 1;

title: Count Logins Per User
problem_statement:
Count the number of login events for each user.

Return user_id, login_count. Order by login_count DESC.
hint: GROUP BY user_id, COUNT.
explanation: Count login activity per user.
solution_sql: SELECT user_id,COUNT(*)AS login_count FROM logins GROUP BY user_id ORDER BY login_count DESC;

title: Monthly Revenue for Restaurants
problem_statement:
Calculate total order revenue per month for restaurants.

Return month, total_revenue. Order by month.
hint: SUBSTR(order_date,1,7) + SUM(amount) GROUP BY.
explanation: Group orders by year-month and sum amounts.
solution_sql: SELECT SUBSTR(order_date,1,7)AS month,SUM(amount)AS total_revenue FROM orders GROUP BY SUBSTR(order_date,1,7) ORDER BY month;

title: Doctors With 10+ Years Experience
problem_statement:
Find doctors who have 10 or more years of experience.

Return name, specialty, experience_yrs. Order by experience_yrs DESC.
hint: WHERE experience_yrs >= 10.
explanation: Dr. Sharma(15), Dr. Patel(10).
solution_sql: SELECT name,specialty,experience_yrs FROM doctors WHERE experience_yrs>=10 ORDER BY experience_yrs DESC;

title: Customers Who Placed 2+ Orders
problem_statement:
Find customers who have placed 2 or more orders.

Return name, order_count. Order by order_count DESC.
hint: JOIN + GROUP BY + HAVING COUNT >= 2.
explanation: Ankit has 3 orders. Others have 1.
solution_sql: SELECT c.name,COUNT(o.id)AS order_count FROM customers c JOIN orders o ON c.id=o.customer_id GROUP BY c.name HAVING COUNT(o.id)>=2 ORDER BY order_count DESC;

title: Combine Name and Department
problem_statement:
Create a display string combining name and department like 'Alice (Eng)'.

Return display. Order by name.
hint: Use || for string concatenation.
explanation: Alice (Eng), Bob (Eng), Charlie (Sales), etc.
solution_sql: SELECT name||' ('||dept||')'AS display FROM employees ORDER BY name;

title: Running Total of Salaries
problem_statement:
Calculate a running total of salaries ordered by hire date.

Return name, salary, running_total. Order by hire_date.
hint: SUM(salary) OVER(ORDER BY hire_date).
explanation: Cumulative sum as you go down by hire date.
solution_sql: SELECT name,salary,SUM(salary) OVER(ORDER BY hire_date)AS running_total FROM employees ORDER BY hire_date;

title: Posts With More Than 50 Likes
problem_statement:
Find posts that received more than 50 likes.

Return post_id, content preview (first 30 chars), likes. Order by likes DESC.
hint: WHERE likes > 50.
explanation: Filter high-engagement posts.
solution_sql: SELECT id AS post_id,SUBSTR(content,1,30)AS preview,likes FROM posts WHERE likes>50 ORDER BY likes DESC;

title: Shows Watched by User 1
problem_statement:
Find all shows watched by user with id=1.

Return show title, watch_date. Order by watch_date.
hint: JOIN watch_history + shows WHERE user_id=1.
explanation: Filter watch history for a specific user.
solution_sql: SELECT s.title,w.watch_date FROM watch_history w JOIN shows s ON w.show_id=s.id WHERE w.user_id=1 ORDER BY w.watch_date;

title: Total Transaction Amount Per Account
problem_statement:
Calculate the total transaction amount for each account.

Return holder name, total_amount. Order by total_amount DESC.
hint: JOIN accounts + transactions, SUM(amount), GROUP BY.
explanation: Sum all transactions per account holder.
solution_sql: SELECT a.holder,SUM(t.amount)AS total_amount FROM accounts a JOIN transactions t ON a.id=t.acc_id GROUP BY a.holder ORDER BY total_amount DESC;

title: High-Value Customers Over 10K
problem_statement:
Find customers whose total spending exceeds 10,000.

Return name, total_spent. Order by total_spent DESC.
hint: JOIN + GROUP BY + HAVING SUM > 10000.
explanation: Ankit and Rohan are high-value.
solution_sql: SELECT c.name,SUM(o.total)AS total_spent FROM customers c JOIN orders o ON c.id=o.customer_id GROUP BY c.name HAVING SUM(o.total)>10000 ORDER BY total_spent DESC;

title: Departments With Avg Salary Above 50K
problem_statement:
Find departments where the average salary exceeds 50,000.

Return dept, avg_salary. Order by avg_salary DESC.
hint: GROUP BY dept HAVING AVG(salary) > 50000.
explanation: Eng avg=65K, Sales avg=52.5K. HR avg=45K excluded.
solution_sql: SELECT dept,AVG(salary)AS avg_salary FROM employees GROUP BY dept HAVING AVG(salary)>50000 ORDER BY avg_salary DESC;

title: Employees Not in Engineering
problem_statement:
Find all employees who are NOT in the Engineering department.

Return name, dept. Order by name.
hint: WHERE dept != 'Eng'.
explanation: Charlie(Sales), Diana(Sales), Frank(HR).
solution_sql: SELECT name,dept FROM employees WHERE dept!='Eng' ORDER BY name;

title: Youngest and Oldest Patient
problem_statement:
Find the youngest and oldest patient.

Return type ('Youngest'/'Oldest'), name, age.
hint: Use UNION with MIN and MAX age subqueries.
explanation: Youngest: Priya(28). Oldest: Amit(45).
solution_sql: SELECT 'Youngest'AS type,name,age FROM patients WHERE age=(SELECT MIN(age) FROM patients) UNION ALL SELECT 'Oldest',name,age FROM patients WHERE age=(SELECT MAX(age) FROM patients);

title: Second Highest Salary Per Department
problem_statement:
Find the second highest salary in each department. If a dept has only 1 employee, exclude it.

Return dept, name, salary. Order by dept.
hint: DENSE_RANK PARTITION BY dept ORDER BY salary DESC, filter rank=2.
explanation: Eng: Eve(65K). Sales: Diana(50K). HR has only 1 employee.
solution_sql: SELECT dept,name,salary FROM(SELECT dept,name,salary,DENSE_RANK() OVER(PARTITION BY dept ORDER BY salary DESC)AS rk FROM employees)t WHERE rk=2 ORDER BY dept;

title: Products Ordered More Than Once
problem_statement:
Find products that appear in more than one order.

Return product name, order_count. Order by order_count DESC.
hint: JOIN products + order_items, GROUP BY product, HAVING COUNT DISTINCT order > 1.
explanation: Products appearing across multiple orders.
solution_sql: SELECT p.name,COUNT(DISTINCT oi.order_id)AS order_count FROM products p JOIN order_items oi ON p.id=oi.product_id GROUP BY p.name HAVING COUNT(DISTINCT oi.order_id)>1 ORDER BY order_count DESC;

title: FULL OUTER JOIN: All Employees & Departments
problem_statement:
Use FULL OUTER JOIN to show all employees AND all departments, even if unmatched.

Return employee name (or NULL), department from employees (or NULL), dept_name from departments (or NULL), budget (or NULL). Order by COALESCE(department, dept_name).

⚠️ This problem uses PostgreSQL (FULL OUTER JOIN is not available in SQLite).
hint: FULL OUTER JOIN matches on employee.department = departments.dept_name. Unmatched rows appear with NULLs.
explanation: HR and Finance have no employees. All employees have matching departments.
solution_sql: SELECT e.name,e.department,d.dept_name,d.budget FROM employees e FULL OUTER JOIN departments d ON e.department=d.dept_name ORDER BY COALESCE(e.department,d.dept_name),e.name;

title: FULL OUTER JOIN: Unmatched Only
problem_statement:
Use FULL OUTER JOIN to find departments with no employees AND employees in departments not in the departments table (orphans).

Return dept source (employee department or departments.dept_name), has_employees (boolean), has_dept_record (boolean). Order by dept.

⚠️ PostgreSQL required.
hint: FULL OUTER JOIN + WHERE either side IS NULL to find mismatches.
explanation: HR and Finance have no employees.
solution_sql: SELECT COALESCE(e.department,d.dept_name)AS dept,e.id IS NOT NULL AS has_employees,d.id IS NOT NULL AS has_dept_record FROM employees e FULL OUTER JOIN departments d ON e.department=d.dept_name WHERE e.id IS NULL OR d.id IS NULL GROUP BY COALESCE(e.department,d.dept_name),e.id IS NOT NULL,d.id IS NOT NULL ORDER BY dept;

title: FULL OUTER JOIN: Budget Utilization
problem_statement:
Using FULL OUTER JOIN, compare each department's budget against its actual payroll (SUM of salaries).

Return dept_name, budget, actual_payroll, utilization_pct (rounded to 1). Order by utilization_pct descending NULLS LAST.

⚠️ PostgreSQL required.
hint: FULL OUTER JOIN + GROUP BY + SUM salary vs budget.
explanation: Engineering: 190K salary / 500K budget = 38%. Marketing: 110K / 200K = 55%.
solution_sql: SELECT d.dept_name,d.budget,COALESCE(SUM(e.salary),0)AS actual_payroll,ROUND(COALESCE(SUM(e.salary),0)*100.0/d.budget,1)AS utilization_pct FROM departments d FULL OUTER JOIN employees e ON d.dept_name=e.department GROUP BY d.dept_name,d.budget ORDER BY utilization_pct DESC NULLS LAST;

title: FULL OUTER JOIN: Customer-Order Reconciliation
problem_statement:
Use FULL OUTER JOIN to reconcile customers with their orders. Show ALL customers (even with no orders) and ALL orders (even with orphan customer IDs).

Return customer name (or 'Unknown'), order_id (or NULL), total (or 0). Order by name, order_id.

⚠️ PostgreSQL required.
hint: FULL OUTER JOIN customers to orders, COALESCE for NULLs.
explanation: Some customers may not have orders. Some orders may have invalid customer IDs.
solution_sql: SELECT COALESCE(c.name,'Unknown')AS name,o.id AS order_id,COALESCE(o.total,0)AS total FROM customers c FULL OUTER JOIN orders o ON c.id=o.customer_id ORDER BY name,order_id;

title: FULL OUTER JOIN: Cross-Table Coverage Report
problem_statement:
Create a full coverage report: for every department (from both tables), show the department name, count of employees, budget, and whether it's 'Staffed', 'Empty', or 'Unbudgeted'.

Return dept, emp_count, budget, status. Order by dept.

⚠️ PostgreSQL required.
hint: FULL OUTER JOIN + CASE for status classification.
explanation: Engineering/Marketing/Sales = Staffed. HR/Finance = Empty. No Unbudgeted ones exist.
solution_sql: SELECT COALESCE(e.department,d.dept_name)AS dept,COUNT(e.id)AS emp_count,COALESCE(d.budget,0)AS budget,CASE WHEN COUNT(e.id)>0 AND d.id IS NOT NULL THEN 'Staffed' WHEN COUNT(e.id)=0 AND d.id IS NOT NULL THEN 'Empty' ELSE 'Unbudgeted' END AS status FROM employees e FULL OUTER JOIN departments d ON e.department=d.dept_name GROUP BY COALESCE(e.department,d.dept_name),d.id,d.budget ORDER BY dept;
